question: What would happen to Earth's orbit if the Sun was gently compressed to form a 1 solar mass black hole?

options.A: Earth's orbit would not change.
options.B: Earth's orbit would expand due to the increase in gravity.
options.C: Earth would be pulled toward the black hole by its extreme gravity, eventually settling into a smaller orbit.
options.D: Earth would be pulled toward the black hole by its extreme gravity, spiraling in until it was swallowed by the black hole.
options.E: Earth's orbit would be unchanged, but it would experience intense tidal forces due to the increased spacetime curvature.

explanation: According to General Relativity, gravity is not a force but a curvature of spacetime caused by mass. If the Sun were compressed into a black hole of the same mass, the spacetime curvature at Earth's orbital distance would be identical, so Earth's orbit would remain unchanged. A black hole is not a 'vacuum cleaner' — it only has the same gravitational effect as any other object of equal mass at the same distance.

question: A passenger at the back of a spaceship that is traveling at 0.60c relative to Earth shines a light toward the front of the spaceship. At what speed do the other passengers aboard the spaceship see the light travel, relative to themselves?
sub_topic: 2.2 Constancy of the Speed of Light
explanation: One of the fundamental postulates of special relativity is that the speed of light is the same for all observers regardless of the motion of the source or observer. Passengers aboard the spaceship measure light traveling past them at exactly c, no matter how fast the ship moves relative to Earth. This constancy of c is what leads to all the surprising consequences of special relativity.

question: Identify the difference between apparent brightness and luminosity.

options.A: Apparent brightness doesn't depend on distance; luminosity does depend on distance.
options.B: Apparent brightness is the brightness measured in the visible spectrum; luminosity is the brightness measured across the whole electromagnetic spectrum.
options.C: Apparent brightness is the intrinsic brightness of a star; luminosity is the brightness measured at a fixed distance.
options.D: Apparent brightness is the brightness measured by an observer at some distance from a star; luminosity is the star's intrinsic brightness.
options.E: Apparent brightness is the sum of the brightness measured in all directions; luminosity is the brightness measured in one direction.

explanation: Luminosity is the total intrinsic power output of a star — how much energy it emits per second. Apparent brightness is how bright the star looks to an observer at some distance, and it depends on both the luminosity and the distance via the inverse square law. Two stars with the same luminosity can have very different apparent brightnesses if they are at different distances.

question: In the Twin Paradox, one twin stays on Earth, while the other twin travels away at high speed, turns around, and returns to Earth. When they reunite, which twin is younger, and why?

options.A: The Earth-bound twin is younger because the travelling twin takes a longer 'spacetime path', so the travelling twin's clock records that more time has passed.
options.B: The travelling twin is younger because the travelling twin follows a longer path through spacetime, exchanging motion through time for motion through space.
options.C: The travelling twin is younger because their trip has two moving parts (outbound and return), so they experience double the amount of time dilation.
options.D: The Earth-bound twin is younger because the Earth-bound twin stays in one reference frame while the travelling twin switches frames.
options.E: In the end, the paradox isn't really a paradox at all: both twins age the same amount, but they each 'see' the other twin aging at different rates during the different parts of the traveling twin's journey.

explanation: In the Twin Paradox, the traveling twin undergoes acceleration when turning around, which breaks the symmetry between the two reference frames. The traveling twin ages less because they spend time in a different (non-inertial) reference frame and take a shorter path through spacetime. The asymmetry is resolved by recognizing that only one twin actually changes reference frames.

question: Which is the weakest fundamental force of nature?

options.A: Electromagnetic force.
options.B: Strong nuclear force.
options.C: Weak nuclear force.
options.D: Gravitational force.
options.E: Tidal force.

sub_topic: 8.1 Why the Sun Needs Nuclear Fusion
explanation: The four fundamental forces of nature are gravity, electromagnetism, the strong nuclear force, and the weak nuclear force. Gravity is by far the weakest — it is approximately 10^36 times weaker than electromagnetism. Despite being the weakest force, gravity dominates at large (astronomical) scales because it is always attractive and has infinite range.

question: Astronomers use one telescope to observe two stars, Star A and Star B. They find that the two stars appear equally bright. They measure that Star B's parallax angle is half as large as Star A's. Based on these observations, which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Since they appear equally bright, the two stars must have the same luminosity.
options.B: Star A is closer, so Star A must be more luminous than Star B.
options.C: Star A is farther away, so Star A must be more luminous than Star B.
options.D: Star B is closer, so Star B must be more luminous than Star A.
options.E: Star B is farther away, so Star B must be more luminous than Star A.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: The inverse square law states that apparent brightness decreases with the square of the distance from the source. If Star A appears 25 times brighter than Star B from the same telescope but both have the same luminosity, then Star A must be closer by a factor of 5. Apparent brightness depends on both intrinsic luminosity and distance.

question: Two planets, Planet A and Planet B, are observed. Planet B has twice the mass of Planet A. You measure the escape speed at the same distance above the surface of each planet. Which statement about the measured escape speed is CORRECT?

options.A: Planet B has a lower escape speed because it has a greater mass.
options.B: Planet B has a higher escape speed because it has a greater mass.
options.C: Planet B has a higher escape speed because it has a greater density.
options.D: Planet B's escape speed is half of Planet A's because Planet B has double the mass.
options.E: Both planets have the same escape speed because the distance from the center of each planet is the same.

explanation: The Schwarzschild radius is the critical radius to which an object must be compressed to become a black hole. It is directly proportional to mass, so a planet with twice the mass has twice the Schwarzschild radius. Planet B, having twice the mass of Planet A, would need to be compressed to twice the Schwarzschild radius of Planet A to become a black hole.

question: How is nuclear fusion different from a chemical reaction?

options.A: Nuclear fusion results in heavier atoms whereas chemical reactions result in lower mass atoms.
options.B: Nuclear fusion happens when atoms exchange electrons whereas chemical reactions happen when chemical bonds are formed and broken.
options.C: Nuclear fusion requires very high densities whereas chemical reactions require very high temperatures.
options.D: Nuclear fusion involves combining molecules whereas chemical reactions involve combining atoms.
options.E: Nuclear fusion happens when atomic nuclei are combined whereas chemical reactions happen when electrons are rearranged between atoms.

explanation: Chemical reactions involve interactions between electrons in the outer shells of atoms, changing chemical bonds but leaving atomic nuclei unchanged. Nuclear fusion, by contrast, involves combining atomic nuclei under extreme temperatures and pressures, converting mass directly into energy according to E = mc². The energy released per reaction in nuclear fusion is millions of times greater than in chemical reactions.

question: Which of the following is an accurate way to describe why the Moon orbits Earth, according to general relativity?

options.A: The Moon orbits Earth because Earth has a very high escape speed.
options.B: The Moon orbits Earth because they each occupy a different reference frame, and the two reference frames are travelling at a speed relative to one another.
options.C: The Moon orbits Earth because Earth's gravity makes a kind of 'funnel' or 'cone' in spacetime into which the Moon has fallen.
options.D: The Moon orbits Earth because it is following the shortest possible path through curved spacetime.
options.E: The Moon orbits Earth because it isn't moving fast enough to overcome the force of gravity between them.

explanation: According to General Relativity, mass curves spacetime, and objects in free fall follow the straightest possible paths (geodesics) through that curved spacetime. The Moon orbits Earth not because it is being pulled by a force, but because it is following a geodesic through the curved spacetime around Earth's mass. This is the key insight of Einstein's general theory of relativity.

question: A planet is gradually compressed so that its radius becomes smaller. Eventually, the escape speed at its surface reaches the speed of light. Which statement is correct about the object at this stage?

options.A: The object forms an event horizon and becomes a black hole.
options.B: The object develops a singularity whose size is given by the Schwarzschild radius.
options.C: The object's gravity becomes infinite at that radius, requiring objects to move faster than the speed of light.
options.D: The object collapses to form a singularity, which is a point of infinite mass.
options.E: The mass of the object becomes concentrated in a spherical surface of infinite density, called the event horizon.

explanation: A black hole forms when an object is compressed below its Schwarzschild radius, at which point the escape speed exceeds the speed of light. The Schwarzschild radius depends only on mass, so a planet compressed to a small enough size would become a black hole. Once an object crosses inside its Schwarzschild radius, not even light can escape.

question: You measure the spectrum of Star A and Star B, and you find that Star B is hotter than Star A. The two stars are the same size. Based on this observation alone, which of the following is a correct statement?

options.A: Star B is overall redder in colour than Star A.
options.B: Star B has a lower luminosity than Star A.
options.C: Star B is brighter than Star A at red wavelengths.
options.D: Star B is dimmer than Star A at blue wavelengths.
options.E: Star B and Star A are not at the same distance from the observer.

sub_topic: 10.3 Spectral Type as a Temperature Indicator
explanation: Spectral type is directly tied to the surface temperature of a star. Hotter stars (types O and B) show fewer hydrogen absorption lines because high temperatures ionize hydrogen, removing the electrons needed to produce those lines. Since Star B is hotter, its hydrogen lines will be weaker compared to Star A at lower temperature.

question: Barbara is a space pilot flying a cargo ship at 0.99c. Ajay is on Earth. At the moment she passes Earth, they each look at each other's clocks through telescopes. What do they see?

options.A: They each see the other's clock running faster than their own.
options.B: They each see the other's clock running slower than their own.
options.C: Barbara sees Ajay's clock running slower than hers. Ajay sees Barbara's clock running faster than his.
options.D: Barbara sees Ajay's clock running faster than hers. Ajay sees Barbara's clock running slower than his.
options.E: To figure this out, we would need to know the entire path Barbara has followed through spacetime.

sub_topic: 3.2 Motion Through Space vs. Motion Through Time
explanation: According to the spacetime interval, an object moving quickly through space moves more slowly through time — this is time dilation. Barbara traveling at 0.99c will age less than Ajay who remains stationary on Earth. When Barbara returns, Ajay will have aged more, while Barbara will have aged less because she was moving through space at near-light speed.

question: Which of the following is one of the fundamental postulates underlying special relativity?

options.A: The speed of light is the same now as it always has been. This is called the 'invariance of the speed of light'.
options.B: The further an object is away from us, the slower its time appears to move.
options.C: Everyone measures the same speed of light, regardless of how they are moving.
options.D: Massive objects curve space and time, producing gravitational time dilation.
options.E: If an object achieves a high enough density, its spacetime curvature becomes so great that it forms an event horizon.

sub_topic: 2.2 Constancy of the Speed of Light
explanation: Special relativity rests on two foundational postulates: the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames, and the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all inertial observers regardless of the motion of the source. These seemingly simple postulates lead to profound consequences including time dilation and length contraction.

question: The Sun's main energy source is the proton-proton (pp) chain. What is the best explanation for why this process releases energy?

options.A: The pp chain produces unstable helium isotopes that release energy when they undergo radioactive decay.
options.B: Energy is released because gravity causes hydrogen nuclei to collide, converting gravitational energy to heat.
options.C: The energy produced in the pp chain is carried mainly by neutrinos, which escape the Sun easily, carrying energy with them.
options.D: The high temperature in the Sun's core gives photons tremendous energy, and these energetic photons trigger nuclear reactions that produce yet more energy.
options.E: When hydrogen atoms fuse, the mass of the resulting helium nucleus has less mass than the sum of the inputs, and the difference in mass is released as energy.

explanation: The proton-proton chain produces both photons (light) and neutrinos. Photons take hundreds of thousands of years to diffuse out of the Sun's dense interior, while neutrinos — which interact only weakly with matter — escape immediately at nearly the speed of light. Detecting solar neutrinos is therefore the most direct way to study ongoing fusion reactions in the Sun's core.

question: Which of the following observations best provides evidence for the existence of dark matter?

options.A: The high rotational speed of stars in the outer regions of galaxies.
options.B: The existence of supermassive black holes at the center of spiral galaxies.
options.C: The strong gravitational lensing produced by supermassive black holes, such as the one at the heart of the galaxy M87.
options.D: The ability of neutrinos to escape the Sun without interacting with any other matter.
options.E: The precession of the long axis of Mercury's orbit around the Sun.

explanation: Observations of stars orbiting the center of our galaxy (Sagittarius A*) at very high speeds provide strong evidence for a supermassive black hole. By applying Kepler's laws to the observed orbital motions, astronomers can calculate the mass of the central object, which turns out to be about 4 million solar masses confined to an impossibly small region. This is the most compelling evidence for the existence of Sagittarius A* as a black hole.

question: For a star to be on the main sequence, which physical condition must be met?

options.A: The star must have a carbon/oxygen core.
options.B: The star must be in hydrostatic equilibrium.
options.C: The star must be near the centre of the HR diagram.
options.D: The star must be burning light elements such as helium.
options.E: The star must be fusing hydrogen into helium in its core.

explanation: A star is on the main sequence when it is in hydrostatic equilibrium — the outward pressure from nuclear fusion in the core exactly balances the inward pull of gravity. This stable balance can be maintained as long as the star is fusing hydrogen in its core. When core hydrogen is exhausted, the star leaves the main sequence and evolves into a giant.

question: Which of the following correctly describes a fundamental difference between low-mass stars and high-mass stars?

options.A: High-mass stars live longer than low-mass stars because they have more material to fuse.
options.B: High-mass stars can create elements up to iron in their cores, while low-mass stars can only create a few of the lighter elements.
options.C: High-mass stars can become white dwarfs after they die, while low-mass stars become black holes.
options.D: High-mass stars typically form in clusters, while low-mass stars typically form in isolation.
options.E: All high-mass stars get hot enough to begin nuclear fusion reactions in their cores, while not all low-mass stars are able to.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Low-mass stars (like the Sun) can only fuse hydrogen and helium in their cores, ending their lives as white dwarfs. High-mass stars (above ~8 solar masses) are hot enough to fuse progressively heavier elements — carbon, neon, oxygen, silicon — in successive shells, eventually forming an iron core that triggers a supernova. This fundamental difference in endpoint is determined by initial stellar mass.

question: Roughly how many stars does our Milky Way galaxy contain?

options.A: hundreds of thousands
options.B: hundreds of millions
options.C: hundreds of billions
options.D: hundreds of trillions
options.E: hundreds of quadrillions

sub_topic: 16.1 Our Galaxy as a Barred Spiral
explanation: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy containing roughly 100–400 billion stars. It spans about 100,000 light-years in diameter, with most stars distributed in the thin disk, the central bulge, and the spherical stellar halo. Our Sun is located about 26,000 light-years from the galactic center in one of the spiral arms.

question: Which of the following is the most accurate description of a planetary nebula?

options.A: It is the remnant of a supernova explosion.
options.B: It is the remnant of the tidal disruption of a planet.
options.C: It is interstellar gas gathered together by the gravity of a white dwarf.
options.D: It is material ejected from a star at the end of its red giant stage.
options.E: It is the gas and dust that gather around stars as they form, which eventually forms planets.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: A planetary nebula is a shell of gas expelled by a dying low-mass star as it transitions from the asymptotic giant branch to a white dwarf. The star's outer layers are gently expelled and ionized by ultraviolet radiation from the hot core, creating a glowing nebula. The name is misleading — planetary nebulae have nothing to do with planets; early astronomers simply thought they resembled planets through small telescopes.

question: Which of the following elements CANNOT be produced in significant quantities by stable nuclear fusion in the core of a star?

options.A: Iron
options.B: Helium
options.C: Uranium
options.D: Oxygen
options.E: Carbon

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Iron sits at the peak of the nuclear binding energy curve, meaning it has the most tightly bound nucleus. Fusing elements heavier than iron requires an input of energy rather than releasing it, so stars cannot produce elements heavier than iron through ordinary fusion. Elements heavier than iron are primarily produced in neutron star mergers and during supernova explosions through rapid neutron capture (r-process).

question: Why do stars typically expand and turn red late in their life cycles? (Midterm 2)

options.A: Stars typically get larger and cooler as they age because of naturally occurring processes.
options.B: Intense hydrogen shell fusion causes the outer layers of the star to expand, which causes them to cool down.
options.C: As a star creates heavier and heavier elements through nuclear fusion, these heavy elements increase the opacity of the outer layers, causing expansion and cooling.
options.D: Once electron degeneracy pressure takes over a star, it cannot shrink any further, so it must expand. The expansion causes cooling.
options.E: As stars age, the rate of nuclear fusion in their cores increases, so they expand and heat up.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: When core hydrogen is exhausted, hydrogen shell burning begins around the inert helium core. The energy released from this shell pushes the outer layers outward, causing the star to expand dramatically. As the outer envelope expands, it cools — according to Wien's law, cooler objects emit redder light — transforming the star into a red giant.

question: Why don't white dwarfs collapse under their own gravity?

options.A: Electron degeneracy supports white dwarfs against gravity.
options.B: Nuclear fusion in the core of a white dwarf creates strong temperatures and pressures that support the star against gravity.
options.C: Leftover thermal energy from billions of years of nuclear fusion creates enough pressure to support a white dwarf against gravity.
options.D: White dwarfs don't require very much support against gravity because they have low masses compared to main-sequence stars.
options.E: White dwarfs do actually slowly contract under the influence of gravity and will eventually undergo Type Ia supernovae.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: White dwarfs are prevented from collapsing by electron degeneracy pressure — a quantum mechanical effect arising from the Pauli exclusion principle, which prevents two electrons from occupying the same quantum state. This pressure does not depend on temperature and provides permanent support against gravity. White dwarfs have masses below the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) where degeneracy pressure can hold.

question: Which of the following is NOT an accurate description of the ages of populations of stars in the Milky Way?

options.A: The galactic disk contains both young and old stars.
options.B: The galactic center contains mainly younger stars.
options.C: The galactic halo contains mainly older stars.
options.D: Globular clusters consist of mainly older stars.
options.E: Open clusters consist of mainly younger stars.

sub_topic: 12.4 Star Cluster Age Dating (Main Sequence Turnoff)
explanation: Disk stars and halo stars have different average ages because halo stars formed early in the galaxy's history while disk stars formed more recently and continue to form today. Halo stars are generally older and more metal-poor, while disk stars tend to be younger with higher metallicity. This can be measured via the main-sequence turnoff point in star cluster HR diagrams.

question: Which of the following stars would be located in the upper-right region of the H-R diagram?

options.A: A white dwarf
options.B: A G-type main sequence star
options.C: A K-type supergiant star
options.D: An M-type main sequence star
options.E: An O-type main sequence star

explanation: The upper-right region of the HR diagram corresponds to stars that are both cool (red) and extremely luminous. These are red giants and supergiants — stars with low surface temperatures but enormous radii that give them high total luminosities. A cool, luminous star must be very large because the Stefan-Boltzmann law means a cool star needs a very large surface area to have high luminosity.

question: An astronomer observes stars from Cluster A and Cluster B. Cluster A's main sequence extends to higher luminosities and temperatures than Cluster B's. What can the astronomer conclude?

options.A: The clusters are approximately the same age because they both contain main sequence stars.
options.B: Cluster B is older because it has more stars in the red giant region.
options.C: Cluster B is younger because its main sequence extends to cooler temperatures.
options.D: Cluster A is older because it has more massive stars on its main sequence.
options.E: Cluster A is younger because it has more massive stars on its main sequence.

sub_topic: 12.4 Star Cluster Age Dating (Main Sequence Turnoff)
explanation: The main-sequence turnoff point of a star cluster reveals its age: more massive (hotter, bluer) stars evolve off the main sequence first. If Cluster A's main sequence extends to bluer (hotter) stars than Cluster B's, then Cluster A's most massive stars haven't yet evolved off the main sequence, meaning Cluster A is younger. An older cluster has had more time for massive stars to die, leaving a turnoff at fainter, cooler stars.

question: Why can't stars generate energy by fusing elements heavier than iron?

options.A: Stars can generate energy using iron, but only by splitting it apart.
options.B: Fusing iron produces neutrinos that escape and carry energy out of the core, preventing further fusion from occurring.
options.C: Fusion of elements heavier than iron requires higher temperatures and densities than stars are able to produce.
options.D: Fusing elements heavier than iron consumes more energy than it produces.
options.E: Elements heavier than iron are too rare in stellar cores to contribute significantly to energy generation.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.3 Iron Core Formation and the Binding Energy Curve
explanation: Nuclear fusion releases energy only when the product nucleus is more tightly bound than the reactants. Iron-56 sits at the peak of the binding energy curve — it is the most stable nucleus. Fusing iron into heavier elements requires energy input rather than releasing it, so a stellar core that has reached iron can no longer generate energy and will collapse.

question: What causes the 'onion-layer' structure inside a massive star just before it explodes?

options.A: Fusion occurs in layers, with each layer fusing a different element at progressively higher temperatures.
options.B: Fusion occurring in the outer parts of the star causes heavy elements to 'rain down' on the core, assembling layers of progressively heavier elements.
options.C: Each layer of the star formed at a different time during the protostellar phase, with heavier elements settling inward first.
options.D: Convection currents in the star naturally separate different elements into distinct layers based on their atomic mass.
options.E: Elements arrange themselves into layers based on their nuclear stability, with iron (the most stable element) settling at the centre.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: As a massive star ages, successive fusion stages occur at increasingly higher temperatures, but only in the innermost regions where conditions are hot enough. Earlier fusion products accumulate in shells around the core, with each new stage occurring in the center. This creates a layered 'onion' structure with the heaviest elements (iron) in the center and progressively lighter elements in shells further out.

question: Astronomers studying the Bullet Cluster used X-ray telescopes and gravitational lensing to observe the aftermath of a massive collision between two galaxy clusters. What did this observation demonstrate about the nature of dark matter?

options.A: Dark matter and normal matter were both slowed down by the collision as particles of each interacted with one another.
options.B: Dark matter is composed of ordinary material such as black dwarfs, black holes, and rogue planets that are too dim to observe directly.
options.C: Dark matter can interact with itself through a 'dark force', similar to how normal matter interacts electromagnetically.
options.D: Dark matter behaves as predicted by modified theories of gravity, suggesting it isn't actually a new kind of matter after all.
options.E: Dark matter doesn't interact with itself or with normal matter through the electromagnetic force.

sub_topic: 17.5 The Bullet Cluster as Corroborating Evidence
explanation: In the Bullet Cluster, two galaxy clusters collided and passed through each other. The hot gas (detected in X-rays) was slowed by electromagnetic interactions, while the dark matter (mapped by gravitational lensing) passed through without interaction. The separation of the lensing mass from the visible gas demonstrates that most of the clusters' mass is in a non-electromagnetic, collisionless component — dark matter.

question: Why do high-mass stars have much shorter main sequence lifespans than low-mass stars, even though they contain more hydrogen fuel?

options.A: High-mass stars lose most of their hydrogen fuel through stellar winds during their main sequence lifetimes.
options.B: High-mass stars fuse hydrogen inefficiently, extracting less energy from the same amount of fuel than low-mass stars.
options.C: High-mass stars convert their hydrogen into heavier elements that cannot be fused, which wastes fuel.
options.D: High-mass stars consume their hydrogen at a much higher rate to support themselves against their stronger gravity.
options.E: High-mass stars have convective cores that continuously remove hydrogen from the fusion region, reducing their lifespans.

explanation: High-mass stars have much shorter main-sequence lifetimes because they burn through their hydrogen fuel far faster than low-mass stars. Although they have more fuel, they are far more luminous, consuming fuel at a rate that greatly exceeds their mass advantage. The lifetime scales roughly as M/L ∝ M^(-2.5), so a 10 solar mass star lives roughly 300 times shorter than the Sun.

question: Which of the following best describes the matter content of the universe today? (MT2)

options.A: Most of the universe's mass consists of dark matter, while ordinary matter makes up only a small fraction.
options.B: Most of the matter in the universe is ordinary matter made primarily of protons, neutrons and electrons, but much of it is hidden in cold gas or faint stars that are difficult to observe.
options.C: Most of the universe's mass consists of things that don't emit much light, such as planets, neutrinos, and black holes, which we collectively call 'dark matter'.
options.D: About 35% of all matter currently in the universe is contained within stars.
options.E: Over 90% of the matter in the universe today was produced inside stars through nuclear fusion over billions of years.

sub_topic: 1.2 Composition of the Universe (Normal Matter, Dark Matter, Dark Energy)
explanation: Modern cosmological measurements (notably from the Planck satellite) show the universe is approximately 68% dark energy, 27% dark matter, and only about 5% normal (baryonic) matter. Normal matter — everything made of atoms, including stars, planets, and gas — is a small minority of the universe's total energy content. This composition is surprising because almost everything we can directly observe constitutes less than 5% of the universe.

question: Which of the following pieces of information about a star would be needed to calculate the star's luminosity?

options.A: Apparent brightness and distance
options.B: Apparent brightness and apparent magnitude
options.C: Surface temperature and distance
options.D: Surface temperature and apparent brightness
options.E: Blackbody spectrum and surface temperature

explanation: The luminosity of a star can be calculated using the Stefan-Boltzmann law: L = 4πR²σT⁴, which depends on both radius and surface temperature. To calculate luminosity, you need to know both the radius (or size) and the surface temperature of the star. Spectral type alone gives temperature but not size; distance alone gives neither.

question: In the Twin Paradox, one twin stays on Earth, while the other travels away at high speed and returns. When they reunite, which twin is younger, and why? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: The Earth-bound twin is younger because the travelling twin takes a longer path through spacetime, so the travelling twin's clock records that more time has passed.
options.B: The Earth-bound twin is younger because the Earth-bound twin stays in one reference frame while the travelling twin switches frames.
options.C: The travelling twin is younger because their trip has two moving parts (outbound and return), so they experience double the amount of time dilation.
options.D: The travelling twin is younger because moving through space reduces how much time passes on their clock between departure and reunion.
options.E: In the end, the paradox isn't really a paradox at all: both twins age the same amount, but they each 'see' the other twin aging at different rates during the different parts of the traveling twin's journey.

explanation: The Twin Paradox appears paradoxical because each twin might claim the other is moving, implying each should see the other age less. The resolution is that the situation is not symmetric: the traveling twin must accelerate and change reference frames when turning around, while the stay-at-home twin remains in an inertial frame. Only the traveling twin ages less, and this is confirmed by experiment.

question: A passenger at the front of a spaceship that is traveling at 0.98c relative to Earth shines a light toward the back of the spaceship. At what speed do the other passengers aboard the spaceship see the light travel, relative to themselves?
sub_topic: 2.2 Constancy of the Speed of Light
explanation: Special relativity's second postulate states that the speed of light is c for all observers regardless of their motion. A passenger at the front of a spaceship moving at 0.98c would still measure light approaching them at exactly c. The apparent paradox is resolved by time dilation and length contraction, which ensure that all observers measure the same speed of light.

question: What would happen to Earth's orbit if the Sun was replaced with a 1 solar mass black hole? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: Earth would be pulled toward the black hole by its extreme gravity, spiraling in until it was swallowed by the black hole.
options.B: Earth would be pulled toward the black hole by its extreme gravity, eventually settling into a smaller orbit.
options.C: Earth's orbit would expand due to the increase in gravity.
options.D: Earth's orbit would not change.
options.E: Earth's orbit would be unchanged, but it would experience intense tidal forces due to the increased spacetime curvature.

explanation: If the Sun were replaced by a 1 solar mass black hole, the gravitational field at Earth's orbital distance would be identical — the same mass produces the same curvature of spacetime. Earth would continue to orbit at the same distance and speed, unchanged. The common misconception is that black holes have stronger gravity than ordinary objects, but a black hole of the same mass has the same gravitational effect at large distances.

question: Planet A and Planet B have the same mass. Planet A has a radius of 8,000 km while Planet B has a radius of 6,000 km. You measure the escape speed at 10,000 km from the center of each planet. Which statement is CORRECT?

options.A: The escape speed measured near Planet A will be larger because the measuring point is closer to its surface.
options.B: The escape speed measured near Planet A will be larger than that measured near Planet B because it is physically larger.
options.C: The escape speed measured near Planet A will be smaller because the measurement point is farther from its center of mass.
options.D: The escape speed measured near Planet A will be smaller because Planet A is less dense.
options.E: The measured escape speeds will be equal because the two planets have the same mass and you are measuring at the same distance from each.

explanation: The Schwarzschild radius is proportional to mass: r_s = 2GM/c². If both planets have the same mass, their Schwarzschild radii are equal. Planet A (radius 8,000 km) and Planet B (radius 4,000 km) are both larger than their Schwarzschild radii since even Earth's Schwarzschild radius is less than 1 cm, so neither is a black hole. Planet B being smaller doesn't make it a black hole; only compression below its Schwarzschild radius would.

question: You measure the spectrum of Star A and Star B, and you find that Star B is hotter than Star A. The two stars are the same size. Based on this observation alone, which is a CORRECT statement? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: Star B and Star A are not at the same distance from the observer.
options.B: Star B has a lower luminosity than Star A.
options.C: Star B is overall redder in colour than Star A.
options.D: Star B is less bright than Star A at blue wavelengths.
options.E: Star B is brighter than Star A at red wavelengths.

sub_topic: 10.4 Ionization and Molecular Lines at Different Temperatures
explanation: Stellar spectra contain absorption lines from different elements and ions depending on temperature. Hotter stars (like B-type stars) have more ionized atoms and show lines from ions (e.g., He II, singly ionized helium), while cooler stars show neutral atomic lines or even molecular lines. Star B being hotter than Star A means it will show more ionization features.

question: How is nuclear fusion different from a chemical reaction? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: Nuclear fusion happens when atoms exchange electrons, whereas chemical reactions happen when chemical bonds are formed and broken.
options.B: Nuclear fusion happens when atomic nuclei are combined, whereas chemical reactions happen when electrons are rearranged between atoms.
options.C: Nuclear fusion results in heavier atoms, whereas chemical reactions result in lower mass atoms.
options.D: Nuclear fusion requires very high densities, whereas chemical reactions require very high temperatures.
options.E: Nuclear fusion involves combining molecules, whereas chemical reactions involve combining atoms.

explanation: Nuclear fusion involves the merging of atomic nuclei and converts nuclear binding energy into other forms, following E = mc². Chemical reactions involve electrons rearranging between atoms or molecules, involving electromagnetic energy in electron orbitals. The key difference is that fusion operates on nuclei directly and releases millions of times more energy per reaction than chemical processes.

question: Which of the following is an accurate way to describe why the Moon orbits Earth, according to general relativity? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: The Moon orbits Earth because they each occupy a different reference frame, and the two reference frames are travelling at a speed relative to one another.
options.B: The Moon orbits Earth because Earth has a very high escape speed.
options.C: The Moon orbits Earth because it is following the shortest possible path through curved spacetime.
options.D: The Moon orbits Earth because it isn't moving fast enough to overcome the force of gravity between them.
options.E: The Moon orbits Earth because Earth's gravity makes a kind of 'funnel' or 'cone' in spacetime into which the Moon has fallen.

explanation: In General Relativity, the Moon orbits Earth because Earth's mass curves the surrounding spacetime, and the Moon follows a geodesic — the straightest possible path — through that curved spacetime. There is no gravitational 'force' in the Newtonian sense; the Moon is simply in free fall along a curved spacetime path. This is Einstein's elegant reinterpretation of orbital motion.

question: Astronomers use a telescope on Earth to observe two stars, Star A and Star B. The two stars have the same parallax angle, but Star A appears twice as bright as Star B. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Star A must be closer than Star B.
options.B: Star A must be farther away than Star B.
options.C: Star A must have a lower luminosity than Star B.
options.D: Star A must have a higher luminosity than Star B.
options.E: Since the two stars have the same parallax angle, they must have the same luminosity.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: The inverse square law relates apparent brightness to luminosity and distance: b = L / (4πd²). If both stars have the same apparent brightness but Star B is farther away, then Star B must have a higher intrinsic luminosity to compensate for the greater distance. The farther away a star is, the more luminous it must be to appear equally bright.

question: Barbara is a space pilot flying at 0.99c. Ajay is on Earth. At the moment she passes Earth, they each look at each other's clocks through telescopes. What do they see? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: They each see the other's clock running slower than their own.
options.B: They each see the other's clock running faster than their own.
options.C: Barbara sees Ajay's clock running faster than hers. Ajay sees Barbara's clock running slower than his.
options.D: Barbara sees Ajay's clock running slower than hers. Ajay sees Barbara's clock running faster than his.
options.E: To figure this out, we would need to know the entire path Barbara has followed through spacetime.

sub_topic: 3.2 Motion Through Space vs. Motion Through Time
explanation: According to the spacetime interval, all objects move through spacetime at the same 'speed' — the speed of light. An object at rest moves entirely through time, while an object moving at near-c moves mostly through space and very little through time. Barbara traveling at 0.99c experiences significant time dilation: her clock runs slower relative to Ajay's, so she ages less over the same coordinate time.

question: Mary and Larry are both orbiting the same black hole in stable orbits. Mary is at four times the Schwarzschild radius; Larry is at five times. Which statement applies?

options.A: When Mary looks at the singularity, she is seeing it slightly more recently in time than when Larry looks at it, because she is closer to it.
options.B: Mary's clock runs slower than Larry's clock.
options.C: Larry would see Mary being 'spaghettified' because she is closer to the event horizon.
options.D: They are both orbiting within the accretion disk and will suffer from intense X-ray radiation.
options.E: Both of them are inside the event horizon and on their way to the singularity.

explanation: According to General Relativity, gravity is stronger closer to a massive object because spacetime curvature is greater there. Mary orbiting closer to the black hole is in a region of stronger spacetime curvature and therefore experiences greater gravitational time dilation — her clock runs slower relative to Larry's. She therefore ages more slowly than Larry in the outer, weaker-gravity orbit.

question: Which of the following observations is the best evidence for the existence of dark matter? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: The strong gravitational lensing produced by supermassive black holes, such as the one at the heart of the galaxy M87.
options.B: The existence of supermassive black holes at the center of spiral galaxies.
options.C: The precession of the long axis of Mercury's orbit around the Sun.
options.D: The very high number of neutrinos passing through Earth every second.
options.E: The unexpectedly high speeds with which stars orbit in the outer areas of galaxies.

explanation: The most compelling evidence for dark matter in galaxies comes from rotation curves — measurements of orbital speeds of stars and gas as a function of distance from the galactic center. Instead of declining at large radii as expected from visible mass (Keplerian falloff), rotation curves remain flat, implying large amounts of invisible mass extending far beyond the visible disk. This was pioneered by Vera Rubin in the 1960s–1970s.

question: Which of the following would allow us to study nuclear reactions in the core of the Sun in as close to real-time as possible?

options.A: Studying intense X-rays and gamma rays produced by nuclear reactions in the core of the Sun.
options.B: Carefully studying temperature changes on the surface of the Sun using a telescope on Earth.
options.C: Measuring the number of neutrinos reaching Earth from the Sun each minute.
options.D: Using a space telescope in close orbit over the surface of the Sun to stare directly at the core.
options.E: Using a space-based anti-electron (positron) detector.

explanation: Photons produced in the Sun's core take hundreds of thousands of years to random-walk their way to the surface because the solar interior is so dense and opaque. Neutrinos, however, interact only via the weak nuclear force and can travel through the Sun essentially unimpeded, escaping in about 8 minutes. Detecting solar neutrinos therefore provides the most direct window into current nuclear reactions in the core.

question: Which of the following is one of the fundamental postulates of special relativity? (Makeup MT1)

options.A: Everyone measures the same speed of light, regardless of how they are moving.
options.B: The speed of light is the same now as it always has been. This is called the 'invariance of the speed of light'.
options.C: Massive objects curve space and time, producing gravitational time dilation.
options.D: The further an object is away from us, the slower its time appears to move.
options.E: If an object achieves a high enough density, its spacetime curvature becomes so great that it forms an event horizon.

explanation: Special relativity is built on two postulates: (1) the laws of physics are identical in all inertial (non-accelerating) reference frames, and (2) the speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all observers. These postulates imply that there is no preferred reference frame and no absolute notion of rest. All motion is relative to a chosen reference frame.

question: A tidal force is:

options.A: A version of the electromagnetic force.
options.B: A difference between the forces of gravity felt at two points.
options.C: A force that originates at the event horizon of black hole.
options.D: Any force originating from the Moon.
options.E: The result of the formation of a black hole singularity.

explanation: A tidal force is not a separate fundamental force but rather the difference in gravitational force between two points in an extended object. Near a black hole, the curvature of spacetime is so extreme that the gradient of gravity across even a small object is enormous. This differential pull stretches objects along the radial direction and squeezes them perpendicular to it — a process called 'spaghettification'.

question: Why is it not possible for stars to produce energy by fusing iron or heavier elements?

options.A: Fusing iron would increase the mass per nucleon, which would require energy to be provided.
options.B: Fusing iron would decrease the mass per nucleon, reducing the amount of energy available.
options.C: Fusing iron would increase the mass per nucleon, which is not allowed by the laws of quantum mechanics.
options.D: Fusing iron would require overcoming electron degeneracy pressure, which most stars are not capable of doing until they explode as supernovae.
options.E: Fusing iron would require overcoming neutron degeneracy pressure, which would cause the star to collapse to become a black hole.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.3 Iron Core Formation and the Binding Energy Curve
explanation: Fusion releases energy only when the product nucleus has greater binding energy per nucleon than the reactants. Iron-56 has the highest binding energy per nucleon of all elements — it is the most stable nucleus. Any fusion beyond iron would require energy input rather than releasing it, so a stellar core composed of iron cannot generate more energy through fusion and inevitably collapses.

question: Why do stars typically expand and turn red late in their life cycles? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: Stars typically get larger and cooler as they age because of naturally occurring processes.
options.B: As a star creates heavier and heavier elements through nuclear fusion, these heavy elements increase the opacity of the outer layers, causing expansion and cooling.
options.C: Once electron degeneracy pressure takes over a star, it cannot shrink any further, so it must expand. The expansion causes cooling.
options.D: Intense hydrogen shell fusion causes the outer layers of the star to expand, which causes them to cool down.
options.E: As stars age, the rate of nuclear fusion in their cores increases, so they expand and heat up.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: When a star's core hydrogen is depleted, hydrogen shell burning begins around the helium core. The energy from shell burning heats and expands the outer layers, causing the star to grow into a giant. As the outer layers expand, they cool, shifting the star's emission toward longer (redder) wavelengths according to Wien's displacement law.

question: Which of the following correctly describes a fundamental difference between low-mass stars and high-mass stars? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: High-mass stars live longer than low-mass stars because they have more material to fuse.
options.B: High-mass stars can create elements up to iron in their cores, while low-mass stars can only create a few of the lighter elements.
options.C: High-mass stars can become white dwarfs after they die, while low-mass stars become black holes.
options.D: High-mass stars typically form in clusters, while low-mass stars typically form in isolation.
options.E: All high-mass stars get hot enough to begin nuclear fusion reactions in their cores, while not all low-mass stars are able to.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: Low-mass stars like the Sun end their lives as planetary nebulae and white dwarfs through a gradual process of mass loss. High-mass stars (above ~8 solar masses) end in violent core-collapse supernovae when their iron cores exceed the Chandrasekhar mass limit and collapse in seconds. The difference in endpoints is primarily determined by the star's initial mass.

question: For a star to be on the main sequence, which physical condition must be met? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: The star must have a carbon/oxygen core.
options.B: The star must be in hydrostatic equilibrium.
options.C: The star must be near the centre of the HR diagram.
options.D: The star must be burning light elements such as helium.
options.E: The star must be fusing hydrogen into helium in its core.

explanation: A star is on the main sequence when it is in hydrostatic equilibrium, with the outward radiation pressure from core hydrogen fusion balancing the inward gravitational compression. This is a stable, long-lived phase; the Sun has been on the main sequence for about 4.6 billion years and will remain there for another ~5 billion years. When core hydrogen is exhausted, the star evolves off the main sequence.

question: Which of the following is the most accurate description of a planetary nebula? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: It is material ejected from a star at the end of its red giant stage.
options.B: It is the gas and dust that gather around stars as they form, which eventually forms planets.
options.C: It is the remnant of a supernova explosion.
options.D: It is the remnant of the tidal disruption of a planet.
options.E: It is interstellar gas gathered together by the gravity of a white dwarf.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: A planetary nebula forms when a dying low-mass star on the asymptotic giant branch expels its outer layers in a stellar wind, creating a glowing shell of ionized gas. The hot, exposed core (soon to become a white dwarf) provides ultraviolet radiation that ionizes the ejected gas, causing it to fluoresce. Planetary nebulae are short-lived (thousands of years) and have no connection to actual planets.

question: Why don't white dwarfs collapse under their own gravity? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: Leftover thermal energy from billions of years of nuclear fusion creates enough pressure to support a white dwarf against gravity.
options.B: Nuclear fusion in the core of a white dwarf creates strong temperatures and pressures that support the star against gravity.
options.C: Electron degeneracy supports white dwarfs against gravity.
options.D: White dwarfs don't require very much support against gravity because they have low masses compared to main-sequence stars.
options.E: White dwarfs do actually slowly contract under the influence of gravity and will eventually undergo Type Ia supernovae.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: White dwarfs are supported by electron degeneracy pressure, which arises from quantum mechanics (the Pauli exclusion principle). Electrons resist being compressed into the same quantum state, generating a pressure that does not depend on temperature. This degeneracy pressure permanently halts gravitational collapse for white dwarfs below the Chandrasekhar limit of ~1.4 solar masses.

question: Roughly how many stars does our Milky Way galaxy contain? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: hundreds of thousands
options.B: hundreds of millions
options.C: hundreds of billions
options.D: hundreds of trillions
options.E: hundreds of quadrillions

sub_topic: 16.1 Our Galaxy as a Barred Spiral
explanation: The Milky Way is estimated to contain approximately 100–400 billion stars, most commonly cited as about 200–300 billion. This estimate comes from measuring the total mass of the galaxy's visible components and dividing by a typical stellar mass. The vast majority of these stars are too faint to see individually with the naked eye.

question: Which of the following is an accurate description of the ages of populations of stars in the Milky Way?

options.A: Open clusters consist mainly of stars that have left the main sequence.
options.B: The galactic disk contains the smallest proportion of blue stars.
options.C: Globular clusters consist of mainly blue-white stars.
options.D: The galactic center contains mainly young stars.
options.E: The galactic halo contains mainly older stars.

sub_topic: 12.4 Star Cluster Age Dating (Main Sequence Turnoff)
explanation: Stars in the galactic halo are older on average than those in the galactic disk because the halo formed early in the galaxy's history from the first generations of stars. Disk stars formed more recently from gas enriched by earlier stellar generations. This age difference can be inferred from metallicity (halo stars are more metal-poor) and from the turnoff points of globular cluster HR diagrams.

question: An astronomer observes stars from Cluster A and Cluster B. Cluster A's main sequence turn-off occurs higher on the main sequence than Cluster B's. What can the astronomer conclude?

options.A: The clusters are approximately the same age because they both contain main sequence stars.
options.B: Cluster A is younger because its main sequence turn-off occurs higher on the main sequence.
options.C: Cluster A is older because it has more massive stars on its main sequence.
options.D: Cluster B is older because it has stars in the red giant region.
options.E: Cluster B is younger because its main sequence extends to cooler temperatures, and those are the longest-lived stars.

sub_topic: 12.4 Star Cluster Age Dating (Main Sequence Turnoff)
explanation: The main-sequence turnoff point in a star cluster's HR diagram indicates the cluster's age: the most massive (hottest, brightest) stars turn off the main sequence first. A cluster whose main sequence extends to hotter, bluer stars has had less time for its high-mass stars to evolve, making it younger. Comparing turnoff points between two clusters directly reveals their relative ages.

question: Which of the following stars would be located in the upper-left region of the H-R diagram?

options.A: An O-type main sequence star
options.B: An M-type main sequence star
options.C: A K-type supergiant star
options.D: A G-type main sequence star
options.E: A white dwarf

explanation: The upper-left region of the HR diagram corresponds to stars that are hot (blue) and very luminous. These are hot supergiants — massive stars with high surface temperatures and enormous luminosities. A star in the upper-left must be both very hot (explaining its blue color) and very large (explaining its high luminosity despite the concentrated emission per unit area).

question: Astronomers studying the Bullet Cluster observed the result of a massive collision between two galaxy clusters. What did this observation demonstrate about the nature of dark matter? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: Dark matter can interact with itself through a 'dark force', similar to how normal matter interacts electromagnetically.
options.B: Dark matter is composed of ordinary material such as black dwarfs, black holes, and rogue planets that are not bright enough to observe directly.
options.C: Dark matter doesn't interact with itself or with normal matter through the electromagnetic force.
options.D: Dark matter and normal matter were both slowed down by the collision as particles of each interacted with one another.
options.E: Dark matter behaves as predicted by modified theories of gravity, suggesting it isn't actually a new kind of matter after all.

sub_topic: 17.5 The Bullet Cluster as Corroborating Evidence
explanation: The Bullet Cluster consists of two galaxy clusters that have collided and partially passed through each other. The hot gas detected in X-rays was slowed and displaced by collisions, while gravitational lensing reveals that the bulk of the mass (dark matter) passed through without slowing down. This spatial separation of visible gas from gravitational mass is strong evidence that the dominant mass component is collisionless — i.e., dark matter.

question: What causes the 'onion-layer' structure inside a massive star just before it explodes? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: Convection currents in the star naturally separate different elements into distinct layers based on their atomic mass.
options.B: Each layer of the star formed at a different time during the protostellar phase, with heavier elements settling inward first.
options.C: Elements arrange themselves into layers based on their nuclear stability, with iron (the most stable element) settling at the centre.
options.D: Fusion occurs in layers, with each layer fusing a different element at progressively higher temperatures.
options.E: Fusion occurring in the outer parts of the star causes heavy elements to 'rain down' on the core, assembling layers of progressively heavier elements.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Each successive fusion stage in a massive star requires a higher temperature than the previous one. Heavier elements can only fuse in the hottest, innermost regions of the star. As fusion proceeds inward through carbon, neon, oxygen, and silicon stages, earlier fusion ashes accumulate as shells around the new fusion zone, producing the concentric 'onion' layering seen just before a supernova.

question: Which of the following properties of a star most strongly determines its total lifespan, starting from the zero-age main sequence stage?

options.A: The fraction of helium in its core
options.B: Its mass when it first forms
options.C: Its surface temperature
options.D: Its luminosity class
options.E: Its spectral type

explanation: A star's mass is the single most important parameter determining its properties and fate. Mass determines the central temperature and pressure, which controls the rate of fusion and thus the luminosity, surface temperature, radius, and lifetime. Higher mass stars are hotter, more luminous, shorter-lived, and end in more violent deaths compared to lower mass stars.

question: Which of the following best describes the matter content of the universe today? (Makeup MT2)

options.A: About 35% of all matter currently in the universe is contained within stars.
options.B: Most of the matter in the universe is ordinary matter made primarily of protons, neutrons and electrons, but much of it is hidden in cold gas or faint stars that are difficult to observe.
options.C: Most of the universe's mass consists of things that don't emit much light, such as planets, neutrinos, and black holes, which we collectively call 'dark matter'.
options.D: Most of the universe's mass consists of dark matter, while ordinary matter makes up only a small fraction.
options.E: Over 90% of the matter in the universe today was produced inside stars through nuclear fusion over billions of years.

sub_topic: 1.2 Composition of the Universe (Normal Matter, Dark Matter, Dark Energy)
explanation: Current cosmological observations indicate that ordinary baryonic matter (atoms, stars, planets, gas) comprises only about 5% of the universe's total energy content. Dark matter accounts for about 27%, while dark energy makes up approximately 68%. This understanding comes from measurements of the CMB, galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing, and the accelerating expansion of the universe.

question: If a white dwarf forms from a Sun-like star, what will it mainly be made of?

options.A: hydrogen
options.B: electrons
options.C: iron
options.D: hydrogen and helium
options.E: carbon and oxygen

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: A Sun-like star fuses hydrogen into helium during its main-sequence lifetime, then fuses helium into carbon and oxygen during the red giant and horizontal branch phases. When the star's outer layers are expelled as a planetary nebula, the remaining core — a white dwarf — is composed primarily of carbon and oxygen. The star never achieves the temperatures required to fuse carbon further.

question: Approximately how many stars are there in a galaxy like the Milky Way?

options.A: hundreds of thousands
options.B: hundreds of millions
options.C: hundreds of billions
options.D: hundreds of trillions
options.E: more than can be counted

sub_topic: 16.1 Our Galaxy as a Barred Spiral
explanation: A typical large spiral galaxy like the Milky Way contains roughly 100 to 400 billion stars. Current estimates for the Milky Way's stellar population are around 200–300 billion stars. The majority of these stars are faint, low-mass red and orange dwarf stars that individually contribute little light but account for much of the total stellar mass.

question: If two stars have the same luminosity class, then they must also have the same:

options.A: luminosity
options.B: radius
options.C: mass
options.D: temperature
options.E: none of the above

explanation: In the MK classification system, a star's luminosity class (I for supergiants, III for giants, V for main-sequence dwarfs) indicates where the star falls on the HR diagram at a given temperature. Two stars with the same luminosity class occupy the same region of the HR diagram and must therefore have similar surface temperatures. This is because spectral class encodes both temperature and luminosity class.

question: Compared to low-mass stars, the main-sequence lifetimes of high-mass stars are:

options.A: shorter, because high-mass stars ionizes all of their atoms
options.B: shorter, because high-mass stars have much higher luminosities, relative to their masses
options.C: about the same because high mass stars have higher luminosities, but also more hydrogen to fuse
options.D: longer, because high-mass stars have more fuel
options.E: longer, because high-mass stars can fuse heavy atoms

explanation: High-mass stars are dramatically more luminous than low-mass stars — they burn through their fuel at a much faster rate relative to their total mass. The lifetime of a star is approximately proportional to M/L, and since L scales as roughly M^3.5, lifetimes scale as about M^(-2.5). A star ten times the Sun's mass lives only about 1/300th as long despite having ten times more fuel.

question: Which of the following is a NUCLEAR reaction that will RELEASE energy?

options.A: two elements heavier than iron fuse to produce uranium
options.B: an electron in an iron atom jumps up to a higher energy level
options.C: helium fuses with hydrogen to produce lithium
options.D: an electron in an oxygen atom falls down to a lower energy level
options.E: an electron scatters a photon, causing the photon's wavelength to increase

explanation: Nuclear fusion of light elements (below iron on the binding energy curve) releases energy because the product has greater binding energy per nucleon than the reactants. Fusing elements heavier than iron requires energy input. Among the answer choices, fusing hydrogen into helium (the proton-proton chain) is the nuclear reaction that releases energy, as hydrogen is well below iron on the binding energy curve.

question: Which of the following correctly describes a difference between neutron stars and white dwarfs?

options.A: neutron stars can be pulsars, but white dwarfs cannot
options.B: neutron stars are not supported against gravity by fusion, but white dwarfs are
options.C: neutron stars can't produce supernovae by themselves, but white dwarfs can
options.D: neutron stars are the remains of low-mass stars, while white dwarfs are the remains of high-mass stars
options.E: neutron stars are made mainly of neutrons, while white dwarfs are made mainly of electrons

sub_topic: 15.3 Stellar-Mass Black Holes from Massive Stars
explanation: Neutron stars are supported against gravitational collapse by neutron degeneracy pressure — a quantum mechanical effect similar to electron degeneracy in white dwarfs. Black holes, by contrast, have no such support mechanism; gravity overcomes all known forces and compresses matter to a singularity. The maximum mass a neutron star can have (before collapsing to a black hole) is the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit, roughly 2–3 solar masses.

question: Which of the following is an effect of dark energy?

options.A: the Big Bang
options.B: the slow drift of the Moon away from the Earth
options.C: the acceleration of the expansion of the universe
options.D: the expansion of the universe at a constant rate
options.E: the motion of two galaxies in the Local Group away from one another

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: Dark energy acts as a repulsive force causing the expansion of the universe to accelerate over time. Observational evidence for this came from Type Ia supernovae appearing fainter than expected, implying the expansion has been speeding up. As dark energy continues to drive expansion, galaxies beyond our Local Group will eventually recede faster than light, disappearing from our observable universe.

question: Imagine an alien living in a galaxy a billion light years away from Earth. This alien plots the redshifts of galaxies versus their distances. What does she find?

options.A: That all galaxies far from her are redshifted
options.B: That distant galaxies on one side of her sky are blueshifted, while those on the other side of her sky are redshifted
options.C: That all galaxies far from her are blueshifted
options.D: That there is no overall pattern to the redshifts and blueshifts of galaxies as seen from her location.
options.E: That there are more galaxies on one side of her sky than on the other sky, but they are all redshifted.

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Hubble's Law tells us that more distant galaxies are receding faster from us. An alien in a galaxy 1 billion light-years from Earth would also observe all other galaxies receding from them, with distant ones moving faster — just as we observe. The Big Bang was not an explosion at a single point in space but an expansion of space itself, so every point in the universe appears to be the center of expansion.

question: Which part of the Sun has a temperature of about 6000 K?

options.A: the core
options.B: the radiative zone
options.C: the corona
options.D: the surface
options.E: the halo

explanation: The photosphere is the visible surface of the Sun and has a temperature of approximately 5,500–6,000 K. This is the layer from which most of the Sun's visible light is emitted, and it is where sunspots and granulation are observed. Below the photosphere is the convection zone, and above it is the hotter chromosphere and corona.

question: Which of the following properties of a star can be DIRECTLY measured from Earth, using only a telescope and a camera?

options.A: luminosity
options.B: absolute brightness
options.C: distance
options.D: apparent brightness
options.E: total energy emitted per unit time

explanation: Apparent brightness (how bright a star looks in the sky) is a directly measurable quantity — it simply requires observing the star with a telescope and measuring its flux. Distance, luminosity, radius, and mass all require additional information or indirect methods (parallax, standard candles, etc.). Apparent brightness is the most fundamental directly observable stellar property.

question: Which of the following statements about black holes is FALSE?

options.A: all known black holes are more massive than the Sun
options.B: black holes increase in size when they consume matter or energy
options.C: all black holes are at the centers of galaxies
options.D: more massive black holes produce more spacetime curvature at the same distance from their centers
options.E: there are fewer stellar-mass black holes in the Milky Way than there are stars

explanation: Black holes are not 'cosmic vacuum cleaners' that suck in surrounding matter; they only attract matter that passes within their gravitational influence, like any other massive object. A common false statement is that black holes have more gravity than any other object of the same mass, when in fact they obey the same gravitational laws as other bodies. Their extreme nature only manifests very close to the event horizon.

question: According to our best current understanding of dark matter, it:

options.A: consists of tiny particles which do not interact via the electromagnetic force but do interact via the weak nuclear force and gravity
options.B: can only be found in other galaxies, not in the Milky Way
options.C: consists mainly of cold gas in galaxies and galaxy clusters which does not glow brightly enough to be seen by telescopes
options.D: does not absorb visible light, but can absorb some wavelengths of high-energy X-rays
options.E: does not really exist

explanation: Dark matter does not emit, absorb, or reflect electromagnetic radiation of any kind — it is invisible to all telescopes. It interacts gravitationally with normal matter but not through electromagnetism. Current evidence suggests dark matter is non-baryonic (not made of protons and neutrons) and is likely composed of as-yet-undiscovered particles, possibly WIMPs (Weakly Interacting Massive Particles).

question: According to Hubble's Law:

options.A: all galaxies are moving away from us
options.B: elliptical galaxies have redshifts while spiral galaxies have blueshifts
options.C: the Andromeda Galaxy and the Milky Way are on a collision course
options.D: the spectral lines of most galaxies are redshifted
options.E: for any given galaxy, most of its spectral lines are redshifted

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Hubble's Law states that the recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to its distance: v = H₀d, where H₀ is the Hubble constant (~70 km/s/Mpc). More distant galaxies recede faster because there is more intervening space to expand. This relationship implies that the universe is expanding and points to a beginning — the Big Bang.

question: Which of the following would NOT count as evidence for the existence of dark matter?

options.A: a spiral galaxy has a flat rotation curve far beyond the edge of its luminous disk
options.B: more lensed galaxy arcs can be found in an image of a galaxy cluster than can be accounted for by the mass of the visible matter in the cluster
options.C: stars in the neighbourhood of the Sun orbit the center of our galaxy at approximately the same speed as clouds of gas four times as far from the center
options.D: all of the galaxies in a galaxy cluster are moving around more quickly than can be explained by the forces of gravity between their visible masses
options.E: in an elliptical galaxy, stars orbiting far from the center have longer orbital periods than those closer to the center

explanation: Evidence for dark matter includes flat galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing (including the Bullet Cluster), the dynamics of galaxy clusters, and the large-scale structure of the universe. An observation that would NOT be evidence for dark matter would be something explained entirely by visible (baryonic) matter, such as the presence of bright stars in a galaxy — those are normal matter and do not imply dark matter.

question: A massive star can't fuse significant quantities of elements heavier than iron because:

options.A: massive stars are too cool inside to fuse heavy elements
options.B: the fusion of such heavy elements would produce too much energy and destroy the star, causing a supernova
options.C: electron degeneracy in massive stars prevents them from reaching the required densities
options.D: the fusion of elements heavier than iron consumes energy, so it cannot provide the pressure required to support the star against gravity
options.E: the fusion of iron and heavier elements releases neutrons, which form a neutron star and stop the fusion reactions

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.3 Iron Core Formation and the Binding Energy Curve
explanation: Iron-56 has the maximum binding energy per nucleon — it is the most tightly bound nucleus. Any nuclear reaction involving iron, whether fusion into heavier elements or fission into lighter ones, requires an energy input. A star with an iron core cannot generate energy through fusion and thus collapses, triggering a Type II supernova.

question: Which of the following best describes the Sun's current primary source of energy?

options.A: matter combines with antimatter to produce energy and neutrinos
options.B: hydrogen atoms combine with one another to produce helium, carbon, and energy
options.C: as the core of the Sun shrinks, gravitational potential energy is freed and converted to kinetic energy, which heats it up
options.D: carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen help hydrogen atoms fuse with one another, in a process known as the CNO cycle
options.E: protons combine with one another to produce helium, energy, neutrinos, and antimatter

explanation: The Sun's primary energy source is the proton-proton chain, a series of nuclear fusion reactions that convert four hydrogen nuclei (protons) into one helium-4 nucleus, with the release of energy (mostly as photons and neutrinos). This is the main fusion pathway for stars with masses similar to the Sun. More massive stars use the CNO cycle, which also converts hydrogen to helium but via different intermediate reactions.

question: Which of these is the source of most of the hydrogen in the universe?

options.A: main-sequence stars
options.B: the Big Bang
options.C: supernovae
options.D: the p-p chain
options.E: fission

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.4 Big Bang Nucleosynthesis as Independent Evidence
explanation: The vast majority of hydrogen in the universe was produced during Big Bang nucleosynthesis in the first few minutes after the Big Bang, when conditions were hot and dense enough for protons and neutrons to fuse. Stars consume hydrogen through fusion but produce relatively little new hydrogen. The primordial hydrogen from the Big Bang remains the most abundant element in the universe.

question: Most of the elements heavier than iron in the universe come from:

options.A: main-sequence stars
options.B: the Big Bang
options.C: supernovae
options.D: the p-p chain
options.E: fission

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Elements heavier than iron cannot be produced by ordinary stellar fusion because such reactions are endothermic. Instead, elements heavier than iron are primarily formed through the rapid neutron capture process (r-process) during neutron star mergers and, to some extent, during core-collapse supernovae. The 2017 LIGO detection of a neutron star merger (GW170817) confirmed neutron star mergers as a key site of r-process nucleosynthesis.

question: Consider two distant galaxies, A and B. According to Hubble's Law, if the distance to A is three times that to B, then the redshift of A compared to B should be:

options.A: nine times smaller
options.B: three times smaller
options.C: three times larger
options.D: four times larger
options.E: nine times larger

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: According to Hubble's Law, recession velocity is proportional to distance: v = H₀d. If galaxy B is twice as far as galaxy A, then galaxy B recedes at twice the speed. This linear relationship between distance and recession velocity is a direct consequence of uniform expansion of space — all distances in the universe are growing at the same fractional rate.

question: The main difference between a cosmological redshift and a Doppler shift is that:

options.A: a cosmological redshift is caused by the expansion of space whereas a Doppler shift is caused by motion of objects through space
options.B: a cosmological redshift is a much smaller effect than a Doppler shift
options.C: a cosmological redshift is only theoretical, but Doppler shifts are routinely observed in the spectra of actual stars
options.D: cosmological redshifts are only seen in galaxies and Doppler shifts are only seen in stars
options.E: cosmological redshifts always shift light to the red end of the spectrum, while Doppler shifts are toward the blue end of the spectrum

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: A Doppler shift results from an object's motion through pre-existing space — the source is genuinely moving, and the wavelength shift depends on velocity relative to the observer. A cosmological redshift results from the expansion of space itself stretching the wavelength of light as it travels through the universe. For distant galaxies, most of the redshift is cosmological, not Doppler, in origin.

question: According to Kepler's Laws, if two globular clusters with equal masses orbit a galaxy, the one that will take the least time to complete an orbit will be the one with the:

options.A: smallest semi-major axis
options.B: most stars
options.C: largest semi-major axis
options.D: fewest stars
options.E: largest redshift

sub_topic: 16.4 The Stellar Halo and Globular Clusters
explanation: According to Kepler's Third Law, orbital period increases with distance from the center of mass. If two globular clusters have equal masses and orbit a galaxy at different distances, the one farther out takes longer to complete one orbit. The orbital speed also decreases with distance for a Keplerian system where mass is concentrated at the center, following v ∝ 1/√r.

question: You and your friend Mae are in spaceships. Mae passes you traveling at a constant speed of 60 km/hr. You throw a ball toward her at a speed of 60 km/hr. What will Mae see the ball doing?

options.A: coming toward her at 120 km/hr
options.B: coming toward her at 60 km/hr
options.C: floating stationary (0 km/hr)
options.D: moving away from her at 60 km/hr
