options.E: moving away from her at 120 km/hr

explanation: In special relativity, a moving clock appears to run slower than a stationary clock — this is time dilation. You would observe Mae's clock running more slowly than your own, and she would similarly observe your clock running more slowly from her frame. Both observations are correct within their respective reference frames; there is no absolute time.

question: You and your friend Mae are still in spaceships. Mae passes you traveling at a constant speed of 0.999c. You shine a laser toward her. How will Mae say the laser beam moved?

options.A: away from her at 1.001 c
options.B: away from her at 0.001c
options.C: toward her at a speed of 0.001c
options.D: toward her at a speed of 1.000c
options.E: toward her at a speed of 1.999c

explanation: Length contraction is the relativistic effect by which a moving object appears shorter along the direction of motion when measured by a stationary observer. You would measure Mae's spaceship as shorter than it appears in its own rest frame. Mae would similarly measure your spaceship as contracted. Both observations are correct in their respective reference frames.

question: According to General Relativity, why does Earth orbit the Sun?

options.A: the force of gravity between Earth and the Sun keeps Earth in orbit
options.B: Earth and the Sun are connected by a stream of 'gravitons' which tell Earth to orbit the Sun
options.C: Earth is not moving fast enough to escape the Sun's force of gravity
options.D: Earth is following the straightest possible path it can through spacetime bent by the Sun
options.E: Earth is not actually orbiting the Sun, but it appears to do so because of gravitational lensing caused by the Sun's spacetime curvature

explanation: According to General Relativity, the Sun's mass curves the spacetime around it. Earth moves through this curved spacetime, following the straightest possible path (a geodesic), which happens to be an ellipse. There is no gravitational force pulling Earth toward the Sun; Earth is simply in free fall along its geodesic through curved spacetime.

question: Normally, if two protons tried to fuse, they would be repelled by the electromagnetic force. How do they manage to overcome this repulsion in stars?

options.A: the protons are moving fast enough to push through this electromagnetic repulsion
options.B: protons that are about to fuse are very cold, which means they 'freeze' together
options.C: protons don't actually stick together all on their own; they need a 'helper' atom to mediate the fusion reaction
options.D: one of the protons emits a neutrino at the last second before the collision, allowing it to slow down and stick to the other proton
options.E: protons don't actually fuse together; you have to have a proton fuse with a neutron, which is electrically neutral

explanation: Protons carry positive electric charge, so they repel each other due to the electromagnetic force (Coulomb repulsion). For fusion to occur, protons must be brought close enough together that the strong nuclear force — which is very short range but extremely powerful — overcomes this electromagnetic repulsion. In the Sun, extreme temperatures and pressures (quantum tunneling also helps) allow protons to overcome this barrier and fuse.

question: Which of the following best describes the origin of a white dwarf?

options.A: they are stars that form from planets, when a planet ejects a planetary nebula
options.B: they are the result of a supernova of a star like the Sun
options.C: they are objects that nearly became stars, but failed
options.D: they are what is left after the supernova of a massive star in a binary system
options.E: they are the exposed cores of dead main-sequence stars

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: A white dwarf is the exposed, hot core remaining after a low-mass star (up to ~8 solar masses) expels its outer layers as a planetary nebula. It is made primarily of carbon and oxygen and is supported against collapse by electron degeneracy pressure. White dwarfs have masses below the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) and will eventually cool to become black dwarfs over trillions of years.

question: The three main types of galaxies are:

options.A: spiral, elliptical, and irregular
options.B: dwarf, giant, and spiral
options.C: spiral, elliptical, and dwarf
options.D: giant elliptical, dwarf, and spiral
options.E: dwarf, elliptical, and irregular

explanation: The three main morphological types of galaxies are spiral galaxies (including barred spirals), elliptical galaxies, and irregular galaxies. Lenticular galaxies are sometimes listed as a fourth type, representing an intermediate between spirals and ellipticals. This classification system, known as the Hubble tuning fork diagram, organizes galaxies by their visual appearance and structural properties.

question: A universe with no dark energy and a density just a bit higher than the critical density would:

options.A: expand forever at a constant rate
options.B: expand forever at a decelerating rate
options.C: expand until it reached a maximum size, then maintain that size
options.D: eventually collapse again
options.E: never expand in the first place

topic: TOPIC 22: THE ULTIMATE FATE OF THE UNIVERSE
explanation: A universe with density above the critical density and no dark energy has enough gravitational energy to eventually halt and reverse its expansion. The universe would expand to a maximum size, then contract under gravity, ultimately ending in a 'Big Crunch' — a reverse of the Big Bang. Our universe, however, is accelerating due to dark energy and will not end this way.

question: Which of the following types of light has the longest wavelength?

options.A: infrared light
options.B: radio waves
options.C: visible light
options.D: gamma rays
options.E: x-rays

explanation: The electromagnetic spectrum ranges from very short wavelengths (gamma rays, X-rays) through ultraviolet, visible, infrared, to very long wavelengths (radio waves). Radio waves have the longest wavelengths in the electromagnetic spectrum, followed by microwaves. Visible light, which our eyes detect, is a tiny slice of this much broader spectrum.

question: Which of the following pairs of fundamental forces both have infinite range?

options.A: gravity and the weak nuclear force
options.B: electromagnetism and the strong nuclear force
options.C: gravity and electromagnetism
options.D: the strong nuclear force and gravity
options.E: the strong nuclear force and electromagnetism

sub_topic: 8.1 Why the Sun Needs Nuclear Fusion
explanation: Of the four fundamental forces, gravity and electromagnetism both have infinite range — their effects decrease with distance but never reach zero. The strong and weak nuclear forces, by contrast, are extremely short-range, acting only over distances comparable to the size of an atomic nucleus. Both gravity and electromagnetism follow inverse-square laws that extend to infinite distances.

question: Why is it incorrect to describe black holes as 'cosmic vacuum cleaners'?

options.A: we've never actually seen any evidence of anything being sucked into a black hole
options.B: black holes are too far away for anything to be sucked into them
options.C: only very massive black holes act like 'cosmic vacuum cleaners', and those are very rare
options.D: black holes produce no more gravity than any other object with the same mass
options.E: you'd have to get very close to a black hole's event horizon before you could feel its gravity

explanation: The 'cosmic vacuum cleaner' description is incorrect because black holes do not suck in distant matter any more than a star of the same mass would. At distances well outside the event horizon, the gravitational field of a black hole is identical to that of any other object with the same mass. Only matter that comes within the event horizon is inevitably pulled in; distant matter continues to orbit normally.

question: Compared to an atom or molecule with more energy levels, an atom or molecule with fewer energy levels would:

options.A: emit and absorb more red light
options.B: produce spectral lines with longer wavelengths
options.C: emit more light of shorter wavelengths
options.D: produce a smaller number of spectral lines
options.E: shift the peak of its blackbody spectrum toward the red end of the spectrum

explanation: Each element has a unique set of electron energy levels, producing a unique pattern of spectral lines. An atom with fewer energy levels produces fewer possible electron transitions and therefore fewer spectral lines. This is how astronomers identify specific elements in stellar atmospheres — each element has a 'fingerprint' of spectral lines at characteristic wavelengths.

question: Which of the following best describes the composition of the universe?

options.A: 96% dark energy, 2% dark matter, 2% normal matter
options.B: 70% hydrogen, 20% dark energy, 10% dark matter
options.C: 70% dark energy, 25% dark matter, 5% normal matter
options.D: 25% luminous matter, 25% dark matter, 50% dark energy
options.E: 2% normal matter, 28% dark energy, 70% dark matter

sub_topic: 1.2 Composition of the Universe (Normal Matter, Dark Matter, Dark Energy)
explanation: The universe's total energy content is dominated by dark energy (~68%) and dark matter (~27%), with ordinary baryonic matter making up only ~5%. Of the normal matter, hydrogen and helium make up the vast majority (by mass), with heavier elements only a fraction of a percent. This composition was established by observations of the cosmic microwave background and large-scale structure.

question: If the universe had not begun with a Big Bang and had instead existed forever, which of the following features would it definitely not have?

options.A: a cosmic microwave background
options.B: stars
options.C: dark matter and dark energy
options.D: galaxy clusters
options.E: WIMPs

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: The cosmic microwave background (CMB) was produced about 380,000 years after the Big Bang when the universe cooled enough for atoms to form. In a steady-state universe that always existed, the CMB would not be present because it requires a hot, dense early phase to be created. The uniform temperature of the CMB across the sky is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the Big Bang model.

question: In what way are stars on the horizontal branch similar to stars on the main sequence?

options.A: both are fusing hydrogen in their cores
options.B: both have helium-burning shells
options.C: both are groups of stars that all have the same luminosity as one another
options.D: both are powered primarily by fusion reactions in their cores
options.E: both are part of the lifecycle of the most massive stars

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: Stars on the horizontal branch are fusing helium in their cores, just as main-sequence stars fuse hydrogen in their cores. Both are in a state of hydrostatic equilibrium where core fusion pressure balances gravity. The difference is the fuel: main-sequence stars burn hydrogen, while horizontal branch stars burn helium (helium → carbon, oxygen via the triple-alpha process).

question: Imagine a binary star system: a 2 solar mass red giant with spectral class K and a 10 solar mass main-sequence star with spectral class B. What is unusual about this system?

options.A: the star with spectral class B should have a smaller mass than the one with spectral class K
options.B: a red giant and a main sequence star should never be found together in a binary system
options.C: stars of spectral classes K and B should not be able to exist in the same binary star system
options.D: the red giant should have a larger mass than the main sequence star
options.E: the more massive star should have died before the less massive one

explanation: Binary star systems allow measurement of stellar masses through Kepler's Third Law: the orbital period and semi-major axis of two stars orbiting their common center of mass yields their total mass. A binary consisting of a 2 solar mass red giant (K spectral class) and another star can be analyzed to determine both masses if the orbital parameters are measured. Binary stars are the primary means of directly measuring stellar masses.

question: How would stars formed recently differ from stars formed in the early years of the universe?

options.A: they would have a higher fraction of heavy elements
options.B: they would be physically larger
options.C: they would have much shorter lifetimes
options.D: they would be less massive
options.E: they would be more likely to die as supernovae

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Stars formed early in the universe's history (Population II stars) formed from nearly pure hydrogen and helium — the only elements produced in the Big Bang. Over time, successive generations of stars synthesized heavier elements and dispersed them through supernovae, enriching the interstellar medium. Stars formed recently (Population I stars) like our Sun therefore contain significantly higher abundances of metals (elements heavier than helium).

question: When an electron moves from a lower energy level in an atom to a higher one, it:

options.A: emits light
options.B: reflects light
options.C: transmits light
options.D: releases a neutrino
options.E: absorbs light

explanation: Electrons in atoms occupy discrete energy levels. When an electron absorbs a photon, it moves from a lower energy level to a higher one, gaining energy equal to the photon's energy (E = hf). This process is responsible for absorption lines in stellar spectra: photons of specific wavelengths are absorbed by atoms in the star's atmosphere as electrons jump to higher energy levels.

question: As a result of the fusion of two particular atoms, energy is released. We can be sure that:

options.A: the mass per nucleon of the output was less than that of the inputs
options.B: the mass per nucleon of the inputs was less than that of the output
options.C: the mass per nucleon of the inputs and output was the same
options.D: the input atoms were heavier than iron
options.E: the output atoms were lighter than iron

explanation: When nuclear fusion releases energy, the product nucleus must be lighter than the sum of the reactant masses — the 'missing' mass is converted to energy via E = mc². This is a direct consequence of Einstein's mass-energy equivalence. If a fusion reaction releases energy, the total mass of the products is slightly less than the total mass of the reactants, with the difference appearing as kinetic energy or photons.

question: Which of the following stellar spectral classes should be found commonly in spiral galaxies but very rarely in elliptical galaxies?

options.A: K
options.B: F
options.C: O
options.D: G
options.E: M

explanation: O and B stars are hot, massive, short-lived stars that form in regions of active star formation. In spiral galaxies, star formation is concentrated in the spiral arms where dense gas clouds can collapse. Since O and B stars have lifetimes of only a few million to tens of millions of years, they are found near their birthplaces in the spiral arms and are not spread throughout the galaxy.

question: According to the Big Bang model of the universe, approximately how old is the universe?

options.A: 6000 years
options.B: 10 million years
options.C: 14 billion years
options.D: 46.5 billion years
options.E: infinitely old—the universe has existed forever

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: The age of the universe is approximately 13.8 billion years, as determined from multiple independent measurements including the temperature and fluctuations of the cosmic microwave background, Hubble's constant, and the ages of the oldest stars. This measurement was refined by the Planck satellite's detailed observations of the CMB and is one of the most precisely known cosmological parameters.

question: If we remove the cloud of gas from in front of an incandescent bulb (which was producing an absorption spectrum), which type of spectrum could then be observed?

options.A: Spectrum 1 (emission lines on dark background)
options.B: Spectrum 2 (continuous with additional absorption lines)
options.C: Spectrum 3 (shifted absorption lines)
options.D: Spectrum 4 (continuous spectrum)
options.E: Spectrum 5 (blank)

explanation: When a gas cloud is removed from in front of a continuous (blackbody) spectrum source, the spectrum returns to a pure continuous spectrum. The absorption lines were caused by the intervening cool gas absorbing photons at specific wavelengths. Without the absorbing gas, the full continuous spectrum of the incandescent source is seen with no missing wavelengths.

question: If we added another chemical element to the cloud of gas in front of the incandescent bulb, which type of spectrum could then be observed?

options.A: Spectrum 1 (emission lines on dark background)
options.B: Spectrum 2 (continuous with additional absorption lines)
options.C: Spectrum 3 (shifted absorption lines)
options.D: Spectrum 4 (continuous spectrum)
options.E: Spectrum 5 (blank)

explanation: Adding another element to the absorbing gas cloud adds more electron energy levels with different spacings, producing additional absorption lines at different wavelengths. Each element has a unique set of energy levels and therefore a unique pattern of spectral lines — its 'fingerprint'. The new element would add its own absorption lines without removing the existing ones.

question: If we heated the cloud of gas until it was partly ionized, which spectrum could then be observed?

options.A: Spectrum 1 (emission lines on dark background)
options.B: Spectrum 2 (continuous with additional absorption lines)
options.C: Spectrum 3 (shifted absorption lines)
options.D: Spectrum 4 (continuous spectrum)
options.E: Spectrum 5 (blank)

sub_topic: 10.4 Ionization and Molecular Lines at Different Temperatures
explanation: Partially ionized gas has both neutral atoms and ions, each with different electron configurations and energy levels. This creates a more complex spectrum with absorption lines from both neutral atoms and their ions at different wavelengths. Some spectral lines present in the original spectrum might disappear (if those electrons are stripped away by ionization), while new lines from ions would appear.

question: If we cool the gas down below the temperature that produced the original spectrum, which spectrum could then be observed?

options.A: Spectrum 1 (emission lines on dark background)
options.B: Spectrum 2 (continuous with additional absorption lines)
options.C: Spectrum 3 (shifted absorption lines)
options.D: Spectrum 4 (continuous spectrum)
options.E: Spectrum 5 (blank)

sub_topic: 10.4 Ionization and Molecular Lines at Different Temperatures
explanation: Cooling the gas below the temperature that produced the original absorption spectrum causes atoms to recombine and may allow molecules to form. Molecules have additional vibrational and rotational energy levels not present in single atoms, producing many new absorption lines, particularly in the infrared. The spectrum would show molecular absorption bands in addition to or replacing some atomic lines.

question: Given: Satevis M2I 17 solar masses, Capra G8III 2.7 solar masses, P784 K2V 0.8 solar masses, Sothis A1V 2 solar masses. Which of these stars is most likely to die as a Type II supernova?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

explanation: Main-sequence lifetime depends most strongly on mass — higher mass stars consume their fuel much faster relative to their supply. Satevis (M2I, 17 solar masses) is a supergiant already evolved off the main sequence. Among main-sequence stars, the K2V star P784 (0.8 solar masses) has the longest main-sequence lifetime because low-mass stars are far more fuel-efficient than high-mass stars.

question: Which of the stars (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) has the hottest surface temperature?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

explanation: In the OBAFGKM spectral sequence, O-type stars are the hottest and M-type stars are the coolest. Of the stars listed, Sothis with spectral class A1 is a hot A-type star, making it one of the hotter options. The hottest star among the four listed (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) would be Sothis A1V, since A is much hotter than G, K, or M.

question: Which of the stars (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) has the largest radius?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

explanation: Luminosity class I corresponds to supergiants, which are the largest and most luminous stars. Satevis (M2I) is a red supergiant — M-type supergiants are among the largest stars known by radius. Since radius and luminosity are related by the Stefan-Boltzmann law (L = 4πR²σT⁴), and supergiants have enormous radii, Satevis would have the largest radius despite being the coolest of the listed stars.

question: Which of the stars (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) would have the highest apparent brightness, as seen from Earth?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

explanation: Main-sequence lifetime scales inversely and steeply with mass: t ∝ M^(-2.5) approximately. Among the main-sequence stars listed, the smallest mass corresponds to the longest lifetime. P784 K2V at 0.8 solar masses has the longest main-sequence lifetime of the four stars, while Satevis at 17 solar masses has the shortest (and has already left the main sequence as a supergiant).

question: Which of the stars (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) would appear visibly yellow?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

sub_topic: 11.4 White Dwarfs on the HR Diagram
explanation: White dwarfs are the remnants of low-mass stars and are located in the lower-left of the HR diagram — they are hot but very faint due to their tiny size. On an HR diagram, Capra G8III is a giant and would be above the main sequence; P784 K2V is a main-sequence dwarf. The star that would appear lowest on the HR diagram (lowest luminosity) would be a white dwarf if one were listed, but among the given options the K2V main-sequence dwarf P784 is the least luminous visible star.

question: Which of the stars (Satevis M2I, Capra G8III, P784 K2V, Sothis A1V) would have the longest main-sequence lifetime?

options.A: Satevis
options.B: Capra
options.D: Sothis
options.E: There is insufficient information to be sure.

explanation: Spectral type directly encodes surface temperature in the OBAFGKM sequence. G-type stars like Capra (G8III) have surface temperatures around 5,000–6,000 K, making them yellow-white in color. A1V Sothis is a hot white/blue-white star. P784 K2V is an orange-red dwarf, and Satevis M2I is a red supergiant — the coolest. The star most similar in surface temperature to the Sun (G2V) would be Capra (G8III).

question: A spaceship moves past the Earth at a significant fraction of the speed of light. From the perspective of an observer at rest on Earth, what is seen to occur onboard the spaceship?

options.A: Clocks on the spaceship run slow, and the lengths of objects on the spaceship are stretched in the direction of motion.
options.B: Clocks on the spaceship run fast, and the lengths of objects on the spaceship are normal.
options.C: Clocks run normally, and the lengths of objects on the spaceship are contracted in the direction of motion.
options.D: Clocks on the spaceship run slow, and the lengths of objects on the spaceship are contracted in the direction of motion.
options.E: Clocks on the spaceship run fast, and the lengths of objects on the spaceship are contracted in the direction of motion.

explanation: Length contraction in special relativity means that a moving object appears shorter along its direction of motion as measured by a stationary observer. Observers on Earth would measure the passing spaceship as shorter than it appears in its own rest frame. Simultaneously, due to time dilation, observers on Earth would measure time passing more slowly on the spaceship.

question: Consider the 'twin paradox', in which Zoomer travels to a distant star and then returns, while Stayer remains on Earth. Which of the following statements is true for Stayer?

options.A: Zoomer's clock runs slow for both Zoomer's outbound and inbound journey.
options.B: Zoomer's clock runs normally for both Zoomer's outbound and inbound journey.
options.C: Zoomer's clock runs fast for both Zoomer's outbound and inbound journey.
options.D: Zoomer's clock runs slow for Zoomer's outbound journey, but runs fast for Zoomer's return journey.
options.E: Zoomer's clock runs fast for Zoomer's outbound journey, but runs slow for Zoomer's return journey.

explanation: The Twin Paradox is resolved by recognizing that the twins are not in equivalent situations: Zoomer must accelerate and change reference frames to turn around and return, while Stayer remains in an inertial frame. This breaks the apparent symmetry between the two twins. Zoomer, who experienced more motion through space, ages less than Stayer when they reunite.

question: According to the General Theory of Relativity, which of the following statements correctly describes the relation between mass and spacetime?

options.A: All masses are zero in a reference frame that involves spacetime.
options.B: All velocities are zero in a reference frame that involves spacetime.
options.C: Mass and spacetime are equivalent in all reference frames.
options.D: Massive objects produce curvature in spacetime.
options.E: The acceleration and velocity of massive objects are the same thing in curved spacetime.

explanation: The equivalence principle states that the effects of gravity and acceleration are locally indistinguishable. An observer in a closed box cannot determine by local experiments whether they are in a uniform gravitational field or uniformly accelerating through empty space. This was Einstein's 'happiest thought' and the foundation of General Relativity, leading to the prediction that gravity bends light.

question: Which list best describes the components of a black hole?

options.A: A light year and a neutrino.
options.B: An event horizon and a singularity.
options.C: An event horizon and a neutron star.
options.D: A Hertzsprung-Russell diagram and a singularity.
options.E: An event horizon, a singularity, and a neutron star.

explanation: A black hole's structure consists of the singularity (where density becomes infinite at the center), the event horizon (the boundary from within which not even light can escape, located at the Schwarzschild radius), and the spacetime around it. There are no solid surfaces or physical structures. The Schwarzschild radius marks the event horizon, not a physical shell.

question: Which of the following would NOT occur in the vicinity of a black hole?

options.A: Production of X-rays from an accretion disk.
options.B: The velocity of falling objects exceeding the speed of light.
options.C: Rapid orbiting by surrounding stars.
options.D: Spaghettification of infalling astronauts.
options.E: Gravitational lensing of stars and galaxies behind the black hole.

explanation: Near a black hole, extreme spacetime curvature causes extreme tidal forces that would stretch and compress objects (spaghettification). Light is severely redshifted escaping from near the event horizon (gravitational redshift). However, objects in free fall near a black hole would experience tidal forces, not weightlessness — they are stretched radially and compressed laterally. Events inside the event horizon cannot be observed from outside.

question: Which statement best describes the colour of light that we receive from the Sun?

options.A: The Sun emits most of its light in X-rays.
options.B: The Sun's light is yellow.
options.C: The Sun's light is white.
options.D: The Sun's light is green, but it appears white to our eyes.
options.E: The Sun's light is mostly white, but some specific colours are missing.

explanation: The Sun emits radiation approximating a blackbody spectrum with a peak wavelength determined by its surface temperature (~5,800 K). According to Wien's displacement law, λ_max = b/T, the peak emission of the Sun is in the green-yellow part of the visible spectrum. However, because it emits across all visible wavelengths, our eyes perceive the Sun as white or yellow-white.

question: Which statement correctly describes the Sun's source of energy?

options.A: A hydrogen atom weighs less than a helium atom.
options.B: A hydrogen atom weighs more than a helium atom.
options.C: Four hydrogen atoms weigh less than a helium atom.
options.D: Four hydrogen atoms weigh more than a helium atom.
options.E: Hydrogen is more stable than iron, but helium is less stable than iron.

explanation: The Sun generates energy through nuclear fusion in its core via the proton-proton chain. Four hydrogen nuclei (protons) are converted into one helium-4 nucleus, two positrons, two neutrinos, and energy in the form of gamma rays. The slight mass deficit between four protons and one helium-4 is converted to energy according to E = mc², producing the Sun's luminosity.

question: To determine a star's apparent brightness, we need to know:

options.A: How bright the star appears in the sky.
options.B: The star's parallax.
options.C: The star's luminosity.
options.D: The star's distance.
options.E: The star's parallax and the star's distance.

explanation: Apparent brightness is the flux received at the observer's location and depends only on how much light reaches the observer. To measure apparent brightness directly, you only need to observe the star with a light-collecting instrument (like a telescope) and measure its flux — no knowledge of distance or intrinsic properties is needed. Distance and luminosity are needed to convert apparent brightness to luminosity.

question: Since all stars begin their lives with the same basic composition, what characteristic most determines how they will differ?

options.A: location where they are formed
options.B: time they are formed
options.C: luminosity they are formed with
options.D: mass they are formed with
options.E: color they are formed with

explanation: All stars form with roughly the same chemical composition (mostly hydrogen and helium), so the key parameter that determines a star's properties — temperature, luminosity, radius, lifetime — is its initial mass. Mass sets the central pressure and temperature, which controls the rate of fusion. More massive stars are hotter, more luminous, larger, and shorter-lived than less massive stars.

question: Suppose you see two main-sequence stars of the same spectral type. Star 1 is dimmer in apparent brightness than Star 2 by a factor of 100. What can you conclude?

options.A: Without first knowing the distances to these stars, you cannot draw any conclusions about how their true luminosities compare to each other.
options.B: The luminosity of Star 1 is a factor of 100 less than the luminosity of Star 2.
options.C: Star 1 is 100 times more distant than Star 2.
options.D: Star 1 is 100 times nearer than Star 2.
options.E: Star 1 is 10 times more distant than Star 2.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: Stars of the same spectral type have the same surface temperature and therefore the same luminosity per unit area (same color and temperature). If two such main-sequence stars have the same temperature but different apparent brightnesses, the dimmer one must be farther away. By the inverse square law, if Star 1 is dimmer by a certain factor, it is farther away by the square root of that factor.

question: On a Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, where would we find stars that are cool and luminous?

options.A: upper right
options.B: lower right
options.C: upper left
options.D: lower left

explanation: On the HR diagram, the axes are luminosity (vertical, increasing upward) and surface temperature (horizontal, increasing to the left). Cool, luminous stars occupy the upper-right region — these are red giants and supergiants. Their low temperature means a cool red color, but their enormous size gives them very high luminosity.

question: On the main sequence, stars obtain their energy (2017)

options.A: from chemical reactions.
options.B: from gravitational contraction.
options.C: by converting hydrogen to helium.
options.D: by converting helium to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen.
options.E: from nuclear fission.

explanation: Stars on the main sequence are in the hydrogen-fusing phase of their evolution. The defining characteristic of a main-sequence star is that it fuses hydrogen into helium in its core, with the energy released providing pressure support against gravitational collapse. This is the longest phase in a star's life.

question: On a Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, where on the main sequence would we find stars that have the greatest mass?

options.A: upper right
options.B: lower right
options.C: upper left
options.D: lower left

explanation: On the HR diagram's main sequence, more massive stars are hotter and more luminous, placing them in the upper-left, while less massive stars are cooler and less luminous, in the lower-right. The main sequence runs diagonally from upper-left (hot, bright, massive) to lower-right (cool, faint, low-mass). High-mass O and B stars are in the upper left; low-mass red M dwarfs are in the lower right.

question: At this moment in time, which statement best describes the state of the Sun's core?

options.A: The Sun's core is mostly hydrogen.
options.B: The Sun's core is around 50% hydrogen and 50% helium.
options.C: The Sun's core is mostly helium.
options.D: The Sun's core is around 50% helium and 50% iron.
options.E: The Sun's core is mostly iron.

explanation: The Sun is currently in the middle of its main-sequence lifetime (~4.6 billion years old, with ~5 billion years remaining). It is actively fusing hydrogen into helium in its core via the proton-proton chain, and is in hydrostatic equilibrium. The Sun's core temperature is about 15 million K, hot enough for sustained hydrogen fusion.

question: When the Sun becomes a red giant, how will its position on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram change?

options.A: It will move down and to the left.
options.B: It will move down and to the right.
options.C: It will move up and to the left.
options.D: It will move up and to the right.
options.E: It will move up, but will not move to the left or right.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: When the Sun exhausts its core hydrogen in about 5 billion years, it will expand into a red giant, increasing dramatically in radius while its outer layers cool. On the HR diagram, this evolution moves the Sun to the right (cooler) and upward (more luminous), placing it in the red giant region (upper-right). The surface temperature decreases significantly while total luminosity increases due to the greatly expanded radius.

question: Which of the following will the Sun become at the end of its life?

options.A: An F star.
options.B: A supernova.
options.C: A neutron star.
options.D: A red supergiant.
options.E: A white dwarf.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: The Sun (a 1 solar mass star) will end its life as a white dwarf after passing through the red giant phase, helium flash, horizontal branch, and asymptotic giant branch. It will expel its outer layers as a planetary nebula, leaving the hot, dense carbon-oxygen core as a white dwarf. It will never have sufficient mass to undergo a supernova explosion.

question: Iron will not undergo fusion inside a star because:

options.A: Fusion of iron requires more energy than it produces.
options.B: Fusion of iron produces more energy than it requires.
options.C: There is no atomic element lighter than iron.
options.D: There is no atomic element heavier than iron.
options.E: Iron exists only on planets, not inside stars.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.3 Iron Core Formation and the Binding Energy Curve
explanation: Iron-56 has the highest binding energy per nucleon, meaning it is the most stable nucleus. Fusing iron into heavier elements requires energy input rather than releasing energy — it is endothermic. Therefore, a star with an iron core cannot generate energy through fusion and immediately begins to collapse when the iron core grows beyond the Chandrasekhar mass.

question: In the final stages of the life of a Sun-like star, the star's core is supported against collapse by:

options.A: neutron degeneracy pressure.
options.B: electron degeneracy pressure.
options.C: gravity.
options.D: neutrinos.
options.E: fission.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: In the final stages of a Sun-like star's life, the degenerate core of a white dwarf is supported by electron degeneracy pressure. This quantum mechanical pressure arises because electrons (as fermions) cannot occupy the same quantum state (Pauli exclusion principle), creating a resistance to compression that does not depend on temperature. This distinguishes white dwarfs from ordinary gas pressure-supported objects.

question: What triggers a normal ('Type II') supernova explosion?

options.A: The star turns into a black hole.
options.B: The silicon in the star's core fuses to form iron.
options.C: The protons and electrons in the star's core combine to form neutrons.
options.D: The iron in the star's core fuses to form uranium.
options.E: The iron in the star's core undergoes fission to form silicon.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: A Type II (core-collapse) supernova is triggered when the iron core of a massive star grows beyond the Chandrasekhar mass limit (~1.4 solar masses), at which point electron degeneracy pressure can no longer support it. The core collapses in less than a second to neutron star density, releasing an enormous amount of gravitational potential energy. The outer layers are ejected in the supernova explosion.

question: Conservation of angular momentum means:

options.A: As a star cools down, it spins faster.
options.B: As a star shrinks, it spins faster.
options.C: As a star ages, it spins faster.
options.D: As a star heats up, it spins slower.
options.E: As a star expands, it spins faster.

explanation: Conservation of angular momentum states that angular momentum (L = Iω, where I is the moment of inertia and ω is angular velocity) must be conserved in an isolated system. When a stellar core collapses to form a neutron star, its moment of inertia decreases enormously as its radius shrinks from thousands of km to ~10 km. To conserve angular momentum, the rotational speed (ω) must increase dramatically, which is why neutron stars spin very rapidly.

question: We can detect a neutron star as a pulsar when:

options.A: beams of radiation point along the neutron star's spin axis.
options.B: the neutron star collapses into a black hole.
options.C: beams of radiation sweep over the Earth as the star rotates.
options.D: the neutron star begins to fuse hydrogen to helium in its core.
options.E: the neutron star is smaller than the Schwarzschild radius.

explanation: A pulsar is a neutron star with a strong magnetic field that channels radiation into two narrow beams aligned with its magnetic poles. Because the magnetic axis is often misaligned with the rotation axis, the beams sweep through space like a lighthouse. We can detect the neutron star as a pulsar only when one of these beams is aimed in our direction once per rotation.

question: Which of the following sequences orders objects from lowest density to highest density:

options.A: main sequence star, white dwarf, neutron star, red giant
options.B: neutron star, white dwarf, red giant, main sequence star
options.C: main sequence star, neutron star, red giant, white dwarf
options.D: red giant, main sequence star, white dwarf, neutron star
options.E: white dwarf, red giant, main sequence star, neutron star

sub_topic: 15.3 Stellar-Mass Black Holes from Massive Stars
explanation: From lowest to highest density: white dwarfs have densities around 10^6 g/cm³ (about a million times water density), neutron stars have densities around 10^14–10^15 g/cm³ (nuclear density), and black holes have densities that approach infinity at the singularity. Main-sequence stars have much lower densities than all of these compact remnants.

question: You observe a star in the disk of the Milky Way, and you want to plot the star on an H-R diagram. Which of the following is the one thing NOT needed to make this plot?

options.A: spectral type of the star.
options.B: distance to the star.
options.C: apparent brightness of the star in our sky.
options.D: rotation rate of the star.

explanation: To place a star on an HR diagram, you need its luminosity (total power output) and surface temperature. Luminosity can be determined from apparent brightness and distance (via the inverse square law). Surface temperature can be determined from spectral type or color. These two quantities directly give the star's position on the HR diagram.

question: Which of the following characteristics of stars has the greatest range in values?

options.A: mass
options.B: radius
options.C: core temperature
options.D: surface temperature
options.E: luminosity

explanation: Among all stellar properties, luminosity has the largest range across different types of stars — spanning over 10 orders of magnitude from the faintest brown dwarfs to the most luminous supergiants. Surface temperature ranges only by about a factor of 50, and mass ranges by about a factor of 1000. Luminosity is therefore the property with the greatest variability among stars.

question: How is the lifetime of a star related to its mass?

options.A: More massive stars live slightly shorter lives than less massive stars.
options.B: More massive stars live much longer lives than less massive stars.
options.C: More massive stars live much shorter lives than less massive stars.
options.D: More massive stars live slightly longer lives than less massive stars.

explanation: Stellar lifetime on the main sequence is inversely related to mass in a steep way: t ∝ M/L ≈ M^(-2.5). A star with more mass has more fuel but is far more luminous and burns through its fuel much faster. A 10 solar mass star lives roughly 300 times shorter than the Sun despite having 10 times more hydrogen.

question: What kinds of objects lie in the halo of our galaxy?

options.A: open clusters
options.B: O and B stars
options.C: globular clusters
options.D: gas and dust
options.E: all of the above

sub_topic: 16.4 The Stellar Halo and Globular Clusters
explanation: The galactic halo is a roughly spherical region extending beyond the disk of the Milky Way. It contains old, metal-poor stars and globular clusters — dense, roughly spherical collections of 100,000 to 1 million old stars. Dark matter also extends through and beyond the halo. The halo contains no gas, dust, or ongoing star formation, unlike the disk.

question: Where does most star formation occur in the Milky Way today?

options.A: in the halo
options.B: in the bulge
options.C: in the spiral arms
options.D: in the Galactic centre
options.E: uniformly throughout the Galaxy

explanation: Star formation in the Milky Way today is concentrated in the spiral arms of the galactic disk, where giant molecular clouds of gas and dust are compressed by density waves, triggering collapse and star formation. The galactic center also has some star formation activity. The halo has no significant star formation today as it lacks the cold, dense gas needed.

question: What do we call the bright, sphere-shaped region of stars that occupies the central few thousand light-years of the Milky Way Galaxy?

options.A: the galaxy's disk
options.B: the galaxy's bulge
options.C: a globular cluster
options.D: the galaxy's halo

explanation: The galactic bulge is the bright, spheroidal concentration of stars at the center of a spiral galaxy like the Milky Way. It contains mostly old, metal-rich stars and has a roughly bar-shaped structure in our galaxy (making it a 'barred spiral'). The bulge is distinct from the halo (which is much larger and more diffuse) and from the disk (which is flat and extends beyond the bulge).

question: Why do we believe 90 percent of the mass of the Milky Way is in the form of dark matter?

options.A: The orbital speeds of stars far from the galactic centre are surprisingly high, suggesting that these stars are feeling gravitational effects from unseen matter in the halo.
options.B: Although dark matter emits no visible light, it can be seen at radio wavelengths, and such observations confirm that the halo is full of this material.
options.C: Theoretical models of galaxy formation suggest that a galaxy cannot form unless it has at least 10 times as much matter as we see in the Milky Way disk, suggesting that the halo is full of dark matter.
options.D: Our view of distant galaxies is sometimes obscured by dark blotches in the sky, and we believe these blotches are dark matter located in the halo.

explanation: The rotation curve of the Milky Way shows that stars and gas at large distances from the galactic center orbit at roughly constant speeds rather than declining as expected from the visible mass. By applying Newton's law of gravity to these observed orbital speeds, astronomers can calculate the total enclosed mass, which greatly exceeds the visible mass. The 'missing' mass is attributed to a dark matter halo surrounding the galaxy.

question: The distribution of the dark matter in a spiral galaxy is

options.A: approximately spherical and about the same size as the stellar halo.
options.B: approximately spherical and about ten times the size of the stellar halo.
options.C: flattened in a disk and about the same size as the stellar disk.
options.D: flattened in a disk but about ten times larger than the stellar disk.
options.E: predominantly concentrated in the spiral arms.

explanation: Dark matter in spiral galaxies is distributed in a roughly spherical halo that extends well beyond the visible disk and bulge. Unlike normal matter, which concentrates in the disk, dark matter is distributed in this extended, roughly spherical halo. The halo contains much more total mass than the visible components of the galaxy and explains the flat rotation curves observed.

question: Gravitational lensing occurs when

options.A: massive objects bend light beams that are passing nearby.
options.B: massive objects cause more distant objects to appear much larger than they should and we can observe the distant objects with better resolution.
options.C: dark matter builds up in a particular region of space, leading to a very dense region and an extremely high mass-to-light ratio.
options.D: telescope lenses are distorted by gravity.

explanation: Gravitational lensing occurs when the mass of an intervening object (a galaxy, cluster, or dark matter halo) curves spacetime, bending the path of light from a more distant background source. This allows dark matter to be detected even though it emits no light — it still bends light due to its mass. Strong lensing produces multiple images or Einstein rings; weak lensing produces subtle distortions of background galaxy shapes.

question: Which of the following types of galaxies are most spherical in shape?

options.A: spirals
options.B: ellipticals
options.C: irregulars

explanation: Elliptical galaxies range from perfectly spherical (E0) to quite elongated (E7) in their classification, but they are always smoother and rounder overall compared to spiral or irregular galaxies. Among galaxy types, elliptical galaxies are the most nearly spherical and lack the disk structure of spirals. They contain mostly old, red stars, little gas and dust, and show no spiral arms or disk features.

question: What are peculiar velocities? (2017)

options.A: velocities perpendicular to our line of sight
options.B: velocities directly along our line of sight
options.C: velocities that we cannot explain by only the force of gravity
options.D: apparent velocities caused by the expansion of the universe
options.E: velocities that are not caused by the expansion of the universe

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Peculiar velocities are the actual motions of galaxies through space relative to the overall Hubble flow — they are deviations from the recession velocity predicted by Hubble's Law. A galaxy's observed redshift combines both its cosmological recession (due to expanding space) and its peculiar velocity (true motion through space). Nearby galaxies, like those in the Local Group, can have peculiar velocities large enough to actually appear blueshifted despite the overall expansion.

question: The disk component of a spiral galaxy includes which of the following parts?

options.A: halo
options.B: bulge
options.C: spiral arms
options.D: globular clusters
options.E: all of the above

explanation: The disk of a spiral galaxy contains the spiral arms, young stars, open clusters, and most of the galaxy's gas and dust, which are the ingredients for ongoing star formation. The disk is a flattened, rotating structure that results from the conservation of angular momentum as the galaxy formed. Globular clusters and old halo stars are NOT part of the disk — they belong to the halo.

question: Why are Cepheid variables important?

options.A: Cepheid variables are stars that vary in brightness because they harbour a black hole.
options.B: Cepheids are pulsating variable stars, and their pulsation periods are directly related to their true luminosities. Hence, we can use Cepheids for distance measurements.
options.C: Cepheids are a type of young galaxy that helps us understand how galaxies form.
options.D: Cepheids are supermassive stars that are on the verge of becoming supernovae and therefore allow us to choose candidates to watch if we hope to observe a supernova in the near future.

explanation: Cepheid variable stars pulsate with periods that are directly related to their intrinsic luminosity — more luminous Cepheids pulsate more slowly. By measuring a Cepheid's period, astronomers can determine its true luminosity, and comparing that to its apparent brightness gives the distance via the inverse square law. This period-luminosity relationship, discovered by Henrietta Leavitt, makes Cepheids indispensable standard candles for measuring distances to nearby galaxies.

question: What is a standard candle?

options.A: an object for which we are likely to know the true luminosity
options.B: an object for which we can easily measure the apparent brightness
options.C: any star for which we know the exact apparent brightness
options.D: a long, tapered candle that lights easily

explanation: A standard candle is an astronomical object whose intrinsic luminosity (true brightness) is known independently of its distance. By comparing the known luminosity to the observed apparent brightness, astronomers can use the inverse square law to calculate the distance. Examples include Cepheid variable stars and Type Ia supernovae, which are essential rungs of the cosmic distance ladder.

question: What kinds of objects lie in the disk of our galaxy? (2017)

options.A: open clusters
options.B: O and B stars
options.C: old K and M stars
options.D: gas and dust
options.E: all of the above

explanation: The disk of the Milky Way contains young and middle-aged stars, open clusters, giant molecular clouds, gas, dust, and the spiral arms where new stars are forming. Most of the visible matter in the Milky Way resides in the thin disk, which spans about 100,000 light-years in diameter but is only a few thousand light-years thick. The halo, by contrast, contains old stars, globular clusters, and dark matter but no gas or dust.

question: What is the most accurate way to determine the distance to a nearby star? (2017)

options.A: radar ranging
options.B: stellar parallax
options.C: using Cepheid variables
options.D: Hubble's law

explanation: Stellar parallax is the most direct and accurate method for measuring the distances to nearby stars. It works by measuring the apparent shift in a star's position against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun, providing a geometric (trigonometric) distance. The parallax method is reliable for stars within a few thousand light-years; beyond that, the parallax angle becomes too small to measure accurately.

question: Why can't current theories describe what happened during the Planck era?

options.A: We do not yet have a theory that links quantum mechanics and general relativity.
options.B: We do not understand the properties of antimatter.
options.C: We do not know how much energy existed during that time.
options.D: It was a time period from which we cannot receive radiation.
options.E: The Planck era was the time before the Big Bang, and we cannot describe what happened before that instant.

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
sub_topic: 23.5 The Origin of the Big Bang (Unanswered Question)
explanation: The Planck era refers to the first ~10⁻⁴³ seconds after the Big Bang, when the universe was so hot and dense that quantum gravitational effects dominated. Current physics has no successful theory of quantum gravity — general relativity and quantum mechanics cannot be reconciled at these extremes. Without a theory of quantum gravity, we cannot describe what happened during or before the Planck era.

question: What kinds of atomic nuclei formed during the era of nucleosynthesis?

options.A: only hydrogen
options.B: only helium
options.C: hydrogen and helium and trace amounts of deuterium and lithium
options.D: roughly equal amounts of each of the following: hydrogen, helium, deuterium and lithium
options.E: nuclei of all the chemical elements

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.2 Eras of the Universe (Nucleosynthesis, Nuclei, Atoms)
explanation: During Big Bang nucleosynthesis (the first ~3 minutes after the Big Bang), conditions were hot and dense enough for protons and neutrons to fuse, producing hydrogen nuclei (protons), deuterium, helium-3, helium-4, and a small amount of lithium-7. About 75% of the normal matter emerged as hydrogen and 25% as helium by mass. Heavier elements were not produced in significant quantities because the universe cooled too quickly.

question: Why did the era of nuclei end when the universe was about 380,000 years old?

options.A: All the free particles had combined to form the nuclei of atoms.
options.B: The universe had expanded and cooled to a temperature of about 3,000 K, cool enough for stable, neutral atoms to form.
options.C: Neutrinos and electrons were finally able to escape the plasma of the early universe and no longer heated the other particles.
options.D: Photons were finally able to escape the plasma of the early universe and no longer heated the hydrogen and helium ions.
options.E: No theory can explain this.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.2 Eras of the Universe (Nucleosynthesis, Nuclei, Atoms)
explanation: The era of nuclei ended at about 380,000 years after the Big Bang when the universe cooled to approximately 3,000 K. At this temperature, electrons could finally bind to nuclei without being immediately knocked free by energetic photons, forming neutral atoms — an event called recombination. Before this, the universe was an opaque plasma; after recombination, it became transparent and released the photons we now observe as the Cosmic Microwave Background.

question: Which of the following observations is not a piece of evidence supporting the Big Bang theory?

options.A: darkness of the night sky
options.B: recession speeds of far away galaxies relative to close ones
options.C: observed helium abundance in the universe
options.D: relative motions of galaxies in the Local Group

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: Key evidence for the Big Bang includes the CMB, the observed abundances of light elements (Big Bang nucleosynthesis), and the Hubble expansion. An observation that does NOT support the Big Bang would be anything inconsistent with a hot, dense early universe — for example, if the CMB did not exist, or if the universe were the same age everywhere with no evidence of evolution. The expansion of the universe by itself is consistent with both Big Bang and steady-state models.

question: What do we mean by inflation? (2017)

options.A: the expansion of the universe that we still observe today
options.B: the sudden release of photons when a particle and antiparticle annihilate one another
options.C: a sudden and extremely rapid expansion of the universe that occurred in a tiny fraction of a second during the universe's first second of existence
options.D: quantum fluctuations by high speed, relativistic particles in a state of false vacuum that caused disturbances in the space-time continuum

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: Cosmic inflation refers to a proposed period of extremely rapid, exponential expansion of the universe that occurred in the first tiny fraction of a second (around 10⁻³⁶ to 10⁻³² seconds) after the Big Bang. Inflation would have expanded a tiny, causally connected region to a size much larger than the observable universe today, explaining the remarkable uniformity of the CMB and the flatness of the universe's geometry.

question: Which of the following is true about measurements of the expansion of the Universe?

options.A: The measurements show that the universe may not be expanding at all.
options.B: The measurements show that the universe may be shrinking rather than expanding.
options.C: The measurements show that the expansion is accelerating, rather than slowing under the influence of gravity.
options.D: The measurements indicate that the universe is at least 30 billion years old, meaning that more than 10 billion years passed between the Big Bang and the formation of the first stars and galaxies.
options.E: The data show that the expansion rate varies widely in different parts of the universe.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: Measurements of distant Type Ia supernovae in the late 1990s revealed that the expansion of the universe is accelerating — distant supernovae appeared fainter than expected, implying they were farther away than a decelerating universe would predict. This discovery, which earned the 2011 Nobel Prize in Physics, provided the first direct evidence for dark energy. Independent measurements of the CMB and large-scale structure have since confirmed the accelerating expansion.

question: What might be causing the universe to accelerate in its expansion?

options.A: WIMPs
options.B: neutrinos
options.C: white-dwarf supernovae
options.D: dark gravity
options.E: We don't know!—but we call it 'dark energy.'

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.6 Dark Energy and the Cosmological Constant
explanation: The accelerating expansion of the universe is attributed to dark energy — a mysterious form of energy with negative pressure that acts against gravity on cosmic scales. Dark energy is often modeled as the cosmological constant (Λ) in Einstein's field equations, representing an intrinsic energy density of empty space. Its nature remains one of the greatest unsolved problems in physics.

question: You are in a rocket travelling at 99% of the speed of light. Does the watch on your wrist run fast or slow?

options.A: The watch runs much slower than normal; each tick of the second-hand takes more than a minute.
options.B: The watch runs slightly slower than normal; each tick of the second-hand takes about 1.5 seconds.
options.C: The watch runs at normal speed; each tick of the second-hand takes exactly one second.
options.D: The watch runs slightly faster than normal; each tick of the second-hand takes about 0.7 seconds.
options.E: The watch runs much faster than normal; each tick of the second-hand takes less than a millisecond.

explanation: Time dilation in special relativity means that a moving clock runs slower as measured by a stationary observer, but from the perspective of the person wearing the watch, their own clock always runs normally. You cannot detect your own time dilation — your watch always ticks at one second per second in your own reference frame. The dilation is only apparent when comparing measurements between different reference frames.

question: Einstein's general theory of relativity says that space tells matter how to move. This means that:

options.A: All objects in the Universe are undergoing free-fall.
options.B: All lengths are shorter than they appear.
options.C: Light always travels in perfect straight lines.
options.D: Objects in free-fall follow trajectories determined by curved spacetime.
options.E: Objects move in straight lines when they travel faster than the escape velocity.

explanation: Einstein's General Relativity is often summarized as: mass tells spacetime how to curve, and spacetime tells mass how to move. Objects follow the straightest possible paths (geodesics) through curved spacetime, which we perceive as gravitational attraction. This is fundamentally different from Newton's view of gravity as an instantaneous force acting across flat space.

question: An object sits just outside the event horizon of a black hole. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The object can always escape the black hole, because black holes are not vacuum cleaners.
options.B: The object can escape, provided it moves away at a speed above the escape velocity.
options.C: The object can escape, but only if it first experiences spaghettification.
options.D: The object can probably not escape, because black holes are vacuum cleaners.
options.E: The object cannot escape the black hole under any circumstances.

explanation: The event horizon is the point of no return for a black hole — any object or light that crosses it cannot escape. An object just outside the event horizon is still in a region where escape is theoretically possible, but it would require very high velocity. The event horizon is not a physical surface but an imaginary boundary; an observer falling through it would not feel any special sensation at the moment of crossing.

question: A spectrum is a graph of:

options.A: Luminosity against colour.
options.B: Intensity against wavelength.
options.C: Mass against velocity.
options.D: Speed against angle.
options.E: Force against acceleration.

explanation: A spectrum is a display of the intensity of light as a function of wavelength (or frequency). When astronomers analyze starlight through a spectrograph, they spread the light out by wavelength and record it as a graph showing bright (emission) peaks or dark (absorption) dips at specific wavelengths corresponding to atomic transitions. The pattern of lines serves as a unique fingerprint identifying the chemical composition and temperature of the source.

question: Two stars of the same mass, age and temperature show different sets of absorption lines in their spectra. This is because:

options.A: One star is farther away than the other.
options.B: One star is more luminous than the other.
options.C: The cores of the two stars have different chemical compositions.
options.D: The atmospheres of the two stars have different chemical compositions.
options.E: The situation described is impossible. The two stars must have identical spectra.

explanation: If two stars have the same mass, age, and temperature but show different absorption lines, the most likely explanation is that they have different chemical compositions in their atmospheres. The pattern of absorption lines directly reflects which elements are present and in what ionization states. Different chemical abundances would produce different sets of absorption features even at the same temperature.

question: An astronomer measures the parallax angles of two different stars, star A and star B. The parallax angle of Star A is twice as large as that of Star B. This implies that:

options.A: Star A is twice as close to Earth as Star B.
options.B: Star A is twice as far from Earth as Star B.
options.C: Star A is four times as luminous as Star B.
options.D: Star A is four times less luminous than Star B.
options.E: Star A is twice as old as Star B.

explanation: Parallax angle is inversely proportional to distance: a larger parallax angle means the star is closer. If Star A has a larger parallax angle than Star B, then Star A is closer to Earth. The parsec is defined such that a star with a parallax of 1 arcsecond is at a distance of 1 parsec (about 3.26 light-years), and distance in parsecs = 1/parallax in arcseconds.

question: Which of the following statements about spectral types of stars is true?

options.A: The spectral type of a star can be used to determine its surface temperature.
options.B: The spectral type of a star can be used to determine its color.
options.C: A star with spectral type M is cooler than a star with spectral type O.
options.D: The spectral type of a star can be determined by looking at its spectrum.
options.E: All of the above are true.

sub_topic: 10.3 Spectral Type as a Temperature Indicator
explanation: Spectral type is a direct proxy for surface temperature in the OBAFGKM sequence — O stars are hottest and M stars are coolest. However, spectral type alone does not uniquely determine luminosity, since two stars can have the same temperature but different sizes (e.g., a main-sequence G star vs. a G-type giant). The full luminosity class (added in the MK system) is needed to determine the star's location on the HR diagram.

question: Suppose you see two main-sequence stars of the same spectral type. Star 1 is dimmer in apparent brightness than Star 2 by a factor of 100. What can you conclude? (2018)

options.A: Without first knowing the distances to these stars, you cannot draw any conclusions about how their true luminosities compare to each other.
options.B: The luminosity of Star 1 is a factor of 100 less than the luminosity of Star 2.
options.C: Star 1 is 100 times smaller than Star 2.
options.D: Star 1 is 100 times nearer than Star 2.
options.E: Star 1 is 10 times more distant than Star 2.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: Two main-sequence stars with the same spectral type have the same surface temperature and the same intrinsic luminosity. If Star 1 appears dimmer than Star 2, the only explanation (since luminosity is the same) is that Star 1 is farther away. By the inverse square law, if Star 1 appears 4 times dimmer, it is twice as far away.

question: You observe a star in the disk of the Milky Way, and you want to plot the star on an H-R diagram. You will need to determine all of the following, except the (2018)

options.A: spectral type of the star.
options.B: distance to the star.
options.C: apparent brightness of the star in our sky.
options.D: rotation rate of the star.

explanation: To place a star on an HR diagram, you need its luminosity and surface temperature. Luminosity requires knowing both the apparent brightness and the distance (via the inverse square law). Surface temperature can be inferred from the star's spectral type or color. Both pieces of information are necessary to uniquely determine the star's position on the diagram.

question: What is the diameter of the disk of the Milky Way?

options.A: 100 light-years
options.B: 1,000 light-years
options.C: 10,000 light-years
options.D: 100,000 light-years
options.E: 1,000,000 light-years

explanation: The disk of the Milky Way has a diameter of approximately 100,000 light-years (about 30 kiloparsecs). Our Sun is located about 26,000 light-years (8 kiloparsecs) from the galactic center. The disk is relatively thin — only about 1,000 light-years thick for the thin disk — compared to its enormous diameter.

question: What kinds of objects lie in the disk of our galaxy? (2018)

options.A: open clusters
options.B: O and B stars
options.C: old K and M stars
options.D: gas and dust
options.E: all of the above

explanation: The galactic disk contains young and middle-aged stars of all spectral types, open star clusters, diffuse nebulae, giant molecular clouds, and the interstellar gas and dust from which new stars form. The spiral arms, which are regions of enhanced star formation, are part of the disk. Globular clusters and halo stars are not disk objects — they belong to the spherical halo component.

question: Where does most star formation occur in the Milky Way today? (2018)

options.A: In the halo
options.B: in the bulge
options.C: in the spiral arms
options.D: in the Galactic center
options.E: uniformly throughout the Galaxy

explanation: Active star formation in the Milky Way today occurs primarily in the spiral arms of the disk, where density waves compress giant molecular clouds and trigger gravitational collapse. The galactic center region also has some star formation. The halo does not form new stars today because it lacks the cold, dense molecular gas needed for star formation.

question: What will be the size of the Sun one billion years from now?

options.A: Much smaller than the current size of the Sun.
options.B: About the same as the current size of the Sun.
options.C: Much larger than the current size of the Sun.
options.D: Nobody knows.
options.E: The Sun will not exist in one billion years.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: The Sun is currently about 4.6 billion years old and is roughly halfway through its ~10 billion year main-sequence lifetime. One billion years from now, the Sun will still be on the main sequence, fusing hydrogen in its core and slowly increasing in luminosity. It will be slightly larger and slightly brighter than today, but not yet a red giant — that phase begins in about 5 billion years.

question: At what stage in its evolution will the Sun fuse helium into carbon in its core?

options.A: During the main sequence.
options.B: When the Sun is a red giant.
options.C: When the Sun is a horizontal branch star.
options.D: When the Sun is a white dwarf.
options.E: The Sun will never fuse helium in its core.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: After exhausting core hydrogen, the Sun will become a red giant. Helium core fusion (the triple-alpha process, converting helium to carbon and oxygen) begins in the degenerate helium core with the helium flash, after which the star settles onto the horizontal branch. On the horizontal branch, the Sun's core will be fusing helium into carbon and oxygen, representing the second major phase of core nuclear burning.

question: A white dwarf is very hot and very small. Where is such a star located on a typical Hertzsprung-Russell diagram?

options.A: At top-left.
options.B: At top-right.
options.C: In the centre.
options.D: At bottom-left.
options.E: At bottom-right.

sub_topic: 11.4 White Dwarfs on the HR Diagram
explanation: White dwarfs are hot (high surface temperature) but extremely faint (low luminosity) because they are very small — roughly Earth-sized. On the HR diagram, they plot in the lower-left region: high temperature (left side of the horizontal axis) but low luminosity (bottom of the vertical axis). They are well below the main sequence, separated from it because their small size limits their total power output despite their high temperatures.

question: Are heavy elements like gold made inside the cores of stars?

options.A: Yes, because fusion of gold requires energy rather than produces energy.
options.B: Yes, because fission of gold produces energy rather than requires energy.
options.C: Yes, because fusion of gold produces energy rather than requires energy.
options.D: No, because fission of gold requires energy rather than produces energy.
options.E: No, because fusion of gold requires energy rather than produces energy.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Elements up to iron can be synthesized in stellar cores through successive fusion stages. However, elements heavier than iron (like gold, platinum, and uranium) cannot be produced by stellar fusion because such reactions require energy input. Gold and other very heavy elements are produced primarily through rapid neutron capture (r-process) during neutron star mergers and possibly during some supernovae.

question: When a star goes supernova, the process is:

options.A: Gradual and very faint.
options.B: Gradual and very bright.
options.C: Rapid and very faint.
options.D: Rapid and very bright.
options.E: Unknown, since nobody has seen a supernova since the year 1604.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: A core-collapse supernova is an extraordinarily violent and rapid event — the iron core collapses to neutron star density in less than one second, releasing more energy than the Sun will emit over its entire lifetime. The collapse bounces, sending a shockwave through the infalling outer layers and ejecting them at thousands of kilometers per second. The entire process from core collapse to the ejection of the envelope happens in seconds to hours.

question: Can a white dwarf go supernova?

options.A: No, never.
options.B: Yes, if it is made of helium.
options.C: Yes, if it is of low mass.
options.D: Yes, if it has high angular momentum.
options.E: Yes, if it is in a binary system.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.5 Type Ia Supernova (White Dwarf in Binary)
explanation: A white dwarf in a binary system can accrete mass from a companion star. If the white dwarf's mass reaches the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses), electron degeneracy pressure can no longer support it, and it undergoes a thermonuclear runaway explosion — a Type Ia supernova. This completely destroys the white dwarf, leaving nothing behind. Type Ia supernovae are important standard candles because they all have nearly the same peak luminosity.

question: A neutron star is usually prevented from collapsing into a black hole by:

options.A: the heat from nuclear fission
options.B: the heat from nuclear fusion
options.C: neutron degenerate pressure.
options.D: electron degenerate pressure.
options.E: gravity.

explanation: Neutron stars are supported against gravitational collapse by neutron degeneracy pressure — a quantum mechanical effect arising from the Pauli exclusion principle applied to neutrons. As a result of this pressure, neutrons resist being forced into the same quantum state, providing a pressure that halts collapse. If the neutron star mass exceeds the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit (~2–3 solar masses), even neutron degeneracy pressure fails and the star collapses into a black hole.

question: The fastest known pulsar pulses at a rate of:

options.A: once every 3.4 minutes.
options.B: once every 8.4 seconds.
options.C: 29.9 times per second.
options.D: 716.4 times per second.
options.E: 832,000 times per second.

explanation: Pulsars are rapidly rotating neutron stars, and some of the fastest known pulsars — called millisecond pulsars — pulse hundreds of times per second. The fastest known pulsars pulse at over 700 times per second (over 700 Hz), which is possible because neutron stars are so compact and dense that they can rotate at such extreme rates without flying apart. These millisecond pulsars have been spun up by accreting material from a binary companion.

question: Why do we believe 90 percent of the mass of the Milky Way is in the form of dark matter? (2018)

options.A: The orbital speeds of stars far from the galactic center are surprisingly high, suggesting that these stars are feeling gravitational effects from unseen matter in the halo.
options.B: Although dark matter emits no visible light, it can be seen with radio wavelengths, and such observations confirm that the halo is full of this material.
options.C: Theoretical models of galaxy formation suggest that a galaxy cannot form unless it has at least 10 times as much matter as we see in the Milky Way disk, suggesting that the halo is full of dark matter.
options.D: Our view of distant galaxies is sometimes obscured by dark blotches in the sky, and we believe these blotches are dark matter located in the halo.

explanation: The flat rotation curve of the Milky Way — where stars and gas at large galactic radii orbit at roughly the same speed as those closer in — implies far more mass than is visible. Applying Kepler's laws to the observed orbital speeds gives a total enclosed mass that is about 10 times greater than the visible stellar and gas mass. The excess invisible mass is attributed to a dark matter halo surrounding the galaxy.

question: Gravitational lensing occurs when (2018)

options.A: massive objects bend nearby passing rays of light.
options.B: massive objects cause more distant objects to appear much larger than they should and we can observe the distant objects with better resolution.
options.C: dark matter builds up in a particular region of space, leading to a very dense region and an extremely high mass-to-light ratio.
options.D: telescope lenses are distorted by gravity.

explanation: Gravitational lensing occurs because mass curves spacetime, bending the path of light passing nearby. When a massive object lies between us and a more distant light source, it acts as a gravitational lens, distorting, magnifying, or multiplying the images of background sources. This effect allows astronomers to map the distribution of mass — including invisible dark matter — without relying on its electromagnetic emission.

question: Which of the following types of galaxies are reddest in color?

options.A: Spirals
options.B: Ellipticals
options.C: Irregulars
options.D: They are all about the same

explanation: Elliptical galaxies are the reddest type of galaxy because they are dominated by old, cool, red stars and contain little gas and dust for ongoing star formation. Without young, hot, blue stars (which have short lifetimes and require ongoing star formation), elliptical galaxies have colors dominated by their aging stellar population. Spiral galaxies appear bluer in their disks because of ongoing star formation producing hot O and B stars.

question: Compared to spiral galaxies, elliptical galaxies are (2018)

options.A: redder and rounder.
options.B: redder and flattened.
options.C: bluer and rounder.
options.D: bluer and flattened.
options.E: always much smaller.

explanation: Elliptical galaxies differ from spiral galaxies in several key ways: they contain mostly old, red stars with little gas and dust, and they show no disk structure or spiral arms. They have very little ongoing star formation compared to spiral galaxies and tend to be found more often in dense galaxy clusters. Ellipticals are often described as 'red and dead' compared to the actively star-forming spirals.
