question: The disk component of a spiral galaxy includes which of the following parts? (2018)

options.A: stellar halo
options.B: dark matter halo
options.C: spiral arms
options.D: globular clusters
options.E: all of the above

explanation: The disk component of a spiral galaxy contains the flat, rotating structure including the spiral arms, young stars, open clusters, and most of the interstellar gas and dust. Star formation is ongoing in the disk, particularly in the spiral arms. The halo and bulge are distinct components — globular clusters and old stars belong to the halo, while the central concentration of stars forms the bulge.

question: What is the most accurate way to determine the distance to a nearby star? (2018)

options.A: radar ranging
options.B: stellar parallax
options.C: using Cepheid variables
options.D: Hubble's law

explanation: Stellar parallax is the gold standard for measuring distances to nearby stars because it is purely geometric — it does not rely on any assumptions about the intrinsic properties of the star. By measuring the apparent shift in position of a star against background stars as Earth orbits the Sun, the distance can be calculated trigonometrically. The Hipparcos and Gaia space missions have measured parallaxes for hundreds of millions of stars.

question: What is the most accurate way to determine the distance to a nearby galaxy?

options.A: radar ranging
options.B: stellar parallax
options.C: using Cepheid variables
options.D: Hubble's law

explanation: For nearby galaxies (beyond the range of parallax), the most accurate distance measurement comes from identifying individual Cepheid variable stars. By measuring their pulsation periods, astronomers can determine their intrinsic luminosities, and comparing to apparent brightness gives the distance. The Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope have used Cepheids to measure distances to galaxies tens of millions of light-years away.

question: Which of the following sequences lists the methods for determining distance in the correct order from nearest to farthest?

options.A: parallax, Cepheid variables, Hubble's law
options.B: parallax, Hubble's law, Cepheid variables
options.C: parallax, white-dwarf supernovae, Hubble's law
options.D: parallax, Hubble's law, white-dwarf supernovae

explanation: The cosmic distance ladder builds from nearby to faraway techniques: radar ranging works within the Solar System, stellar parallax works for nearby stars (up to a few thousand light-years), main-sequence fitting and Cepheid variables work for star clusters and nearby galaxies, and Type Ia supernovae and the Tully-Fisher relation work for the most distant galaxies. Each method calibrates the next rung of the ladder.

question: What are peculiar velocities? (2018)

options.A: velocities perpendicular to our line of sight
options.B: velocities directly along our line of sight
options.C: velocities that we cannot explain by only the force of gravity
options.D: velocities caused by the expansion of the universe
options.E: velocities of distant objects that are not caused by the expansion of the universe

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Peculiar velocities are the actual motions of galaxies through space, separate from the Hubble flow (the overall expansion of the universe). They arise from the gravitational attraction of nearby mass concentrations. For nearby galaxies, peculiar velocities can be comparable to or even exceed their Hubble recession velocities, which is why some nearby galaxies (like Andromeda) appear blueshifted.

question: A very distant galaxy has a peculiar velocity towards us. What will the redshift be?

options.A: It will actually be a blue shift
options.B: It will be a greater redshift than predicted by Hubble's law
options.C: It will be a smaller redshift than predicted by Hubble's law
options.D: It will be exactly as predicted by Hubble's law. Peculiar velocity doesn't effect redshift.

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: A very distant galaxy has a large recession velocity from Hubble's Law that dominates over peculiar velocities. Even if a galaxy has a peculiar velocity toward us, its cosmological redshift from Hubble recession at large distances will be much larger. The observed redshift would thus still show a net redshift, but it would be slightly smaller than expected from Hubble's Law alone — the peculiar velocity partially cancels the Hubble recession.

question: Which of these models is ruled out by Hubble's law?

options.A: A universe that is static and unchanging – neither expanding nor contracting.
options.B: A cyclical universe that expands and contracts through a series of big bangs and big crunches.
options.C: An expanding universe that is slowing down and will eventually stop.
options.D: All of these are ruled out by Hubble's law.

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
sub_topic: 20.4 Implications: Expanding Universe and a Beginning
explanation: Hubble's Law shows that all distant galaxies are receding, with recession speed proportional to distance — consistent with a uniformly expanding universe. This rules out a static, eternal universe (the steady-state model with no expansion), because in such a model distant galaxies would not be systematically receding. Hubble's discovery was the key observational evidence against the steady-state theory.

question: Why did the era of nuclei end when the universe was about 380,000 years old? (2018)

options.A: All the free particles had combined to form the nuclei of atoms.
options.B: The universe had expanded and cooled to a temperature of about 3,000 K, cool enough for stable, neutral atoms to form.
options.C: Neutrinos and electrons were finally able to escape the plasma of the early universe and no longer heated the other particles.
options.D: Photons were finally able to escape the plasma of the early universe and no longer heated the hydrogen and helium ions.
options.E: No theory can explain this.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.2 Eras of the Universe (Nucleosynthesis, Nuclei, Atoms)
explanation: At about 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the universe cooled to approximately 3,000 K, allowing protons and electrons to combine into neutral hydrogen atoms (recombination). Before this moment, the universe was a hot ionized plasma opaque to photons; after recombination, the universe became transparent and photons streamed freely. These photons are what we observe today as the Cosmic Microwave Background radiation.

question: Which of the following statements about the Cosmic Microwave Background is true?

options.A: it is red in color and very uniform.
options.B: it has a 3000K thermal spectrum, which was the temperature of the early universe.
options.C: it comes from the time where most of the helium in the universe formed.
options.D: it has a nearly perfect thermal (black body) spectrum.
options.E: all of these are true.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: The Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) is thermal radiation left over from the hot, dense early universe, now cooled to a temperature of about 2.725 K due to the expansion of the universe. It is remarkably uniform across the sky (to 1 part in 100,000), with tiny fluctuations that seeded the formation of galaxies and large-scale structure. The CMB is one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the Big Bang model.

question: What are two key observational facts that led to widespread acceptance of the Big Bang model?

options.A: the cosmic background radiation and the high helium content of the universe
options.B: the cosmic background radiation and the theory of general relativity
options.C: the cosmic background radiation and the near-critical density of the universe
options.D: the predominance of matter over antimatter and the near-critical density of the universe
options.E: the predominance of matter over antimatter and the large-scale structure of galaxies

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: The two key observational facts that led to widespread acceptance of the Big Bang were: (1) the discovery of the Cosmic Microwave Background by Penzias and Wilson in 1965, which matched the predicted thermal radiation from a hot early universe, and (2) the observed abundances of light elements (hydrogen, helium, lithium) matching Big Bang nucleosynthesis predictions. These two independent pieces of evidence strongly supported the Big Bang over competing models.

question: Dark energy is making the universe:

options.A: Expand
options.B: expand at an accelerating rate
options.C: expand more in some directions than in others
options.D: expand at a constant rate, but faster than it would without dark energy
options.E: expand, but the expansion is slowing down

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: Dark energy is causing the expansion of the universe to accelerate — galaxies are flying apart at an ever-increasing rate. This was discovered in 1998 through observations of distant Type Ia supernovae, which appeared fainter (and therefore farther) than expected in a decelerating universe. As dark energy drives continued acceleration, the universe will expand forever, with galaxies beyond our Local Group eventually becoming unreachable.

question: According to our current cosmological models, what is the ultimate fate of the Universe?

options.A: It will collapse again in a super-hot and dense big crunch.
options.B: It will continue to expand, eventually coming to a stop, but the cycle of star formation will continue forever.
options.C: Cold, dark and empty.
options.D: It will end up with 'galaxies' of black holes, neutron stars and white dwarfs, which will last forever.

topic: TOPIC 22: THE ULTIMATE FATE OF THE UNIVERSE
sub_topic: 22.4 Heat Death — Cold, Dark, and Empty Universe
explanation: Given the observed dominance of dark energy and the accelerating expansion, the most likely ultimate fate of the universe is the 'heat death' — an ever-expanding, increasingly cold, dark, and empty universe. Eventually all stars will burn out, black holes will evaporate via Hawking radiation, and the universe will reach maximum entropy with no gradients left to do work. This scenario is sometimes called the 'Big Freeze'.

question: What do we mean by cosmological inflation?

options.A: The Big Bang.
options.B: a sudden exponential expansion of the universe right in the very early universe.
options.C: the expansion of the universe that we still observe today.
options.D: the sudden release of photons when a particle and antiparticle annihilate each other.
options.E: the separation that occurs after two photons collide and create a particle and an antiparticle

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: Cosmological inflation is the hypothesized period of exponentially accelerating expansion in the first ~10⁻³² seconds after the Big Bang, driven by a high-energy quantum field called the inflaton. During inflation, a region smaller than a proton expanded to a size larger than the observable universe today. Inflation explains the flatness of the universe's geometry and the near-perfect uniformity of the CMB.

question: Cosmological Inflation is said to help with the Horizon Problem. What is the Horizon Problem?

options.A: the fact that we can't see further than the era of nuclei with any of our current telescopes.
options.B: the fact that nothing cannot escape a black hole - not even light.
options.C: the fact that the early universe was all at the same temperature, even points too far for light to travel between.
options.D: the fact that galaxies cannot be tracked indefinitely with any one telescope because they set behind the horizon.
options.E: the fact that the accelerating expansion of the Universe will make it cold and dark.

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: The Horizon Problem asks why the CMB has the same temperature in all directions to 1 part in 100,000, even for regions that appear to have been causally disconnected (never in contact with each other) in the standard Big Bang model. Inflation solves this by proposing that all the observable universe was once a tiny, causally connected region that was stretched to enormous size — ensuring all parts had time to reach thermal equilibrium before inflation began.

question: What should happen to light from the end of the Era of Nuclei as it travels to us?

options.A: Cosmological expansion should cause it to become redshifted.
options.B: The peculiar motion of the Milky way should cause it to become redshifted.
options.C: It should fade, because light can't last forever.
options.D: Most of it should get absorbed by gas and dust, so we can no longer see it.
options.E: It should not change at all because the universe is basically transparent.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: Photons released at the end of the Era of Nuclei (at recombination, ~380,000 years after the Big Bang) started as visible/infrared light corresponding to a ~3,000 K blackbody. As the universe has expanded by a factor of ~1,100 since then, these photons have been cosmologically redshifted to microwave wavelengths. We observe them today as the CMB at a temperature of ~2.725 K — the same photons, dramatically stretched to longer wavelengths.

question: In a closed universe (a universe shaped like a 4D sphere), straight and parallel lines eventually converge. What must be true for us to live in a closed Universe?

options.A: there cannot be any dark energy or dark matter.
options.B: there must be more normal (baryonic) matter than either dark matter or dark energy.
options.C: the density of the universe must be larger than the critical density.
options.D: the density of the universe must be smaller than the critical density.

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: In a closed (positively curved) universe, the geometry is analogous to the surface of a sphere in higher dimensions. Parallel lines eventually converge (like longitude lines on Earth meeting at the poles), and the interior angles of a triangle add up to more than 180°. Our universe appears to be very close to flat (Euclidean geometry), which the inflationary model naturally explains by stretching any initial curvature to near-flatness.

question: Astronomers have determined that the star Zeta Toronti is exactly 8 light years from Earth. Rosa is travelling from Earth to Zeta Toronti on a spaceship travelling at 0.2c. According to Rosa, what is the distance between Earth and Zeta Toronto?

options.A: 150 kilometres.
options.B: A little less than 8 light years.
options.C: Exactly 8 light years.
options.D: A little more than 8 light years.
options.E: 40 light years.

explanation: The distance to a star in parsecs is equal to 1 divided by the parallax angle in arcseconds (d = 1/p). A star 8 light-years away is approximately 2.45 parsecs, giving a parallax angle of about 0.41 arcseconds. Alternatively, parallax can be directly related to distance: the closer the star, the larger the parallax angle, and the larger the apparent annual shift in position against background stars.

question: Zoomer and Stayer are identical twins. Zoomer travels to a distant star at high speed, turns around and then returns home; Stayer remains on Earth. Shortly BEFORE Zoomer turns around and begins her journey home, which twin is older?

options.A: They are both the same age.
options.B: The only way to tell is for Zoomer and Stayer to both measure the speed of light.
options.C: Stayer thinks Zoomer is older, and Zoomer thinks Stayer is older.
options.D: Stayer and Zoomer both agree that Zoomer is older.
options.E: Stayer and Zoomer both agree that Stayer is older.

explanation: Zoomer, who travels to a distant star and back at high speed, ages less than Stayer who remains on Earth. This is because Zoomer spends time moving rapidly through space, which means moving more slowly through time — a direct consequence of the spacetime interval. The asymmetry arises because Zoomer must accelerate and change reference frames to turn around, while Stayer remains in an inertial frame throughout.

question: On Earth, Keiji weighs 80 kilograms. While inside a rocket ship, Keiji weighs himself and finds that he weighs 160 kilograms. What can Keiji conclude?

options.A: The rocket ship is in free-fall.
options.B: The rocket ship is coasting.
options.C: The rocket ship has not left Earth.
options.D: The rocket ship has landed on a planet with gravity that is half the strength of Earth's.
options.E: The rocket ship is accelerating.

explanation: Einstein's equivalence principle states that the effects of gravity and acceleration are locally indistinguishable. Keiji in a rocket accelerating at 9.8 m/s² (1g) would feel exactly the same as if he were standing on Earth's surface — he would weigh 80 kg in both cases. If the rocket is accelerating at a different rate than g, he would weigh more or less; the question tells us he still weighs 80 kg, meaning the rocket accelerates at 1g.

question: Correctly complete the following sentence: According to Newton, gravity is _____, but according to Einstein, gravity is _____.

options.A: acceleration; velocity.
options.B: a force; the curvature of spacetime.
options.C: the same reference frame; a change in reference frames.
options.D: angular momentum; nuclear fusion.
options.E: the strongest known force; an illusion.

explanation: Newton described gravity as a force that acts instantaneously between masses through flat space — an 'action at a distance' force. Einstein's General Relativity replaced this with the concept of curved spacetime: mass curves spacetime, and objects follow geodesics through that curved geometry. The key distinction is that Newton treats gravity as a force in flat space, while Einstein treats it as the geometry of curved spacetime.

question: If compressed into an extremely small volume, which of the following substances would form a black hole?

options.A: Hydrogen gas.
options.B: Protons.
options.C: Neutrons.
options.D: Maple syrup.
options.E: All of the above.

explanation: The Schwarzschild radius is proportional to mass: r_s = 2GM/c². Any object compressed below its Schwarzschild radius becomes a black hole. For ordinary objects, this radius is impossibly small — Earth's Schwarzschild radius is about 9mm, and the Sun's is about 3km. A sufficiently massive object compressed to an extremely small volume (below its Schwarzschild radius) would inevitably form a black hole.

question: What is the result when two identical black holes collide and merge?

options.A: A white dwarf.
options.B: A neutron star.
options.C: One black hole, with a smaller Schwarzschild radius than either initial black hole.
options.D: One black hole, with the same Schwarzschild radius as either initial black hole.
options.E: One black hole, with a larger Schwarzschild radius than either initial black hole.

explanation: When two black holes of equal mass merge, conservation of mass-energy and the no-hair theorem dictate the result: a single black hole with roughly twice the mass (minus a small amount radiated away as gravitational waves). The merger releases an enormous amount of energy as gravitational waves — this is precisely what LIGO detected in 2015 (GW150914). The final black hole also has a spin determined by the orbital angular momentum of the merging pair.

question: A spectrum of a star shows three absorption lines. Which of the following conclusions is correct?

options.A: The star's core is composed only of the elements hydrogen and helium.
options.B: The star's core is composed of one element with three energy levels.
options.C: The star's atmosphere is composed only of the elements hydrogen and helium.
options.D: The star's atmosphere is composed of one element with three energy levels.
options.E: The star's atmosphere is a blackbody.

explanation: Three absorption lines in a stellar spectrum indicate the presence of at least one element with those specific electron transition energies, but the number of lines does not directly tell us how many elements are present — one element could produce multiple lines while another might produce only one in the visible range. Additionally, absorption lines tell us about the star's atmosphere, not the interior. The most we can definitively conclude is that at least one element is present that produces those three lines.

question: Which process takes place at the centre of the Sun?

options.A: Nuclear fusion, which converts mass into energy.
options.B: Nuclear fusion, which converts energy into mass.
options.C: Nuclear fission, which converts mass into energy.
options.D: Nuclear fission, which converts energy into mass.
options.E: All of the above.

explanation: At the center of the Sun, the temperature and pressure are high enough (15 million K, 250 billion atmospheres) for nuclear fusion via the proton-proton chain. Four hydrogen nuclei (protons) are combined through several steps into one helium-4 nucleus, releasing energy as gamma rays and neutrinos. This is the primary energy source for the Sun and all main-sequence stars of similar mass.

question: Star A is four times more luminous than Star B. Star A is twice the distance from Earth as Star B. Which statement is correct, for an observer on Earth?

options.A: The apparent brightness of Star A is a quarter the apparent brightness of Star B.
options.B: The apparent brightness of Star A is half the apparent brightness of Star B.
options.C: The apparent brightness of Star A is the same apparent brightness as Star B.
options.D: The apparent brightness of Star A is double the apparent brightness of Star B.
options.E: The apparent brightness of Star A is four times the apparent brightness of Star B.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: Apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance: b = L/(4πd²). Star A is 4 times more luminous than Star B but twice as far away. The apparent brightness ratio is (L_A/L_B) × (d_B/d_A)² = 4 × (1/2)² = 4 × 1/4 = 1. Therefore Star A and Star B have the same apparent brightness as seen from Earth.

question: Which of the following stellar property CANNOT be determined from a star's spectrum?

options.A: Chemical composition.
options.B: Distance.
options.C: Spectral type.
options.D: Colour.
options.E: Temperature.

sub_topic: 10.5 Measuring Multiple Stellar Properties from Spectra
explanation: A star's spectrum reveals its surface temperature (from spectral type/color), chemical composition (from the pattern of absorption lines), radial velocity (from Doppler shifts of spectral lines), and luminosity class (from line widths related to surface gravity). However, a star's distance cannot be determined from its spectrum alone — you need either parallax measurements or knowledge of its intrinsic luminosity combined with its apparent brightness.

question: Star A and Star B are both main sequence stars with the same spectral type. What must be true?

options.A: They have the same luminosity
options.B: They are at the same distance
options.C: They have the same apparent brightness
options.D: Both (b) and (c) must be true
options.E: Without knowing the distances to both stars, we can't conclude anything else.

explanation: Two main-sequence stars with the same spectral type have the same surface temperature, since spectral type is a temperature sequence. On the main sequence, temperature uniquely determines a star's position, so two stars at the same temperature must have the same luminosity, the same radius, and consequently the same mass. This is one of the defining properties of the main sequence: it is a one-parameter family ordered by mass.

question: On a Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, where would we find stars that have the largest radii?

options.A: upper right
options.B: lower right
options.C: upper left
options.D: lower left

explanation: Stellar radius on the HR diagram can be understood through the Stefan-Boltzmann law: L = 4πR²σT⁴. For a given luminosity, stars with lower surface temperatures must have larger radii to compensate. The largest stars are therefore the most luminous and coolest red supergiants, located in the upper-right region of the HR diagram. Stars like Betelgeuse can have radii over 1,000 times that of the Sun.

question: You observe a star in the disk of the Milky Way, and you want to plot the star on an H-R diagram. Which of the following do you NOT need to determine to make this plot? (2019)

options.A: spectral type of the star.
options.B: distance to the star.
options.C: apparent brightness of the star in our sky.
options.D: rotation rate of the star.

explanation: Placing a star on the HR diagram requires knowing its luminosity and surface temperature. Luminosity is derived from the apparent brightness and distance (using the inverse square law), while temperature is determined from spectral type or color. Both quantities are needed to fix the star's unique position on the diagram.

question: On the main sequence, stars obtain their energy (2019)

options.A: from chemical reactions.
options.B: from gravitational contraction.
options.C: by converting hydrogen to helium.
options.D: by converting helium to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen.
options.E: from nuclear fission.

explanation: Stars on the main sequence generate their energy through nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium in their cores. This is the defining characteristic of the main sequence and represents the longest, most stable phase of a star's life. The main sequence on the HR diagram is essentially a hydrogen-fusing sequence, ordered by stellar mass.

question: Consider a 1 solar mass star. What is the main difference between the core of the star while the star is on the main sequence, and the core of the star while it the star is a red giant?

options.A: On the main sequence, the core is pure hydrogen. When the star is a red giant, the core is pure helium.
options.B: On the main sequence, the core is undergoing fusion. When the star is a red giant, the core is not experiencing nuclear reactions.
options.C: On the main sequence, the core is fusing hydrogen. When the star is a red giant, the core is fusing helium.
options.D: On the main sequence, the core is collapsing. When the star is a red giant, the core is expanding.
options.E: There is no real difference between the core on the main sequence and the core of a red giant.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: While a 1 solar mass star is on the main sequence, its core fuses hydrogen in a non-degenerate (ordinary gas pressure) state. After leaving the main sequence and ascending the red giant branch, the helium core becomes degenerate — the electrons are squeezed into their lowest quantum states and provide degeneracy pressure. The helium flash occurs when helium ignites in this degenerate core, after which degeneracy is lifted and the star settles onto the horizontal branch.

question: Consider a 1 solar mass star. In which phase of its life does the star spend the longest amount of time?

options.A: Main sequence.
options.B: Sub-giant.
options.C: Red giant.
options.D: Helium flash.
options.E: Horizontal branch.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: A star spends the vast majority of its life on the main sequence fusing hydrogen in its core — for a 1 solar mass star, this is about 10 billion years. All other phases (red giant, horizontal branch, asymptotic giant branch, planetary nebula) are much shorter by comparison, typically lasting only hundreds of millions of years total. The main sequence is the longest-lived phase because hydrogen fusion is the most energy-efficient stellar power source.

question: What is the final stage of evolution of a 4 solar mass star?

options.A: A core-collapse supernova explosion.
options.B: A black hole.
options.C: A horizontal branch star.
options.D: A red giant.
options.E: A white dwarf.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
sub_topic: 13.7 White Dwarf and Black Dwarf Endpoints
explanation: A 4 solar mass star is above the Sun's mass but below the threshold for a core-collapse supernova (~8 solar masses). It will fuse hydrogen and helium, and possibly carbon, but will eventually lose its outer layers as a planetary nebula, leaving behind a white dwarf. The final white dwarf mass will be below the Chandrasekhar limit, making a white dwarf the final endpoint for this star.

question: Copper has an atomic mass of 63. What produces all the copper in the Universe? (Hint: iron has an atomic mass of 56.)

options.A: Core-collapse supernova explosions.
options.B: Black holes.
options.C: Horizontal branch stars.
options.D: Red giants.
options.E: White dwarfs.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Copper has an atomic mass of 63, which is heavier than iron (56). Since elements heavier than iron cannot be made by stellar fusion in ordinary stellar cores, copper must be produced by neutron capture processes. Copper is primarily produced through the slow neutron capture process (s-process) in the shells of asymptotic giant branch stars and through the r-process in neutron star mergers.

question: Under what conditions can a 1 solar mass star end its life in a supernova explosion?

options.A: When it is composed of helium.
options.B: When it is a red giant.
options.C: When it is supported by electron degeneracy pressure.
options.D: When it is in a binary system.
options.E: A 1 solar mass star can never end its life in a supernova explosion.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.5 Type Ia Supernova (White Dwarf in Binary)
explanation: A 1 solar mass star would normally end its life as a white dwarf. However, if this white dwarf is in a close binary system and accretes enough mass from its companion to reach the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses), it will undergo a thermonuclear runaway explosion — a Type Ia supernova. This is the only scenario under which a star of this mass can explode as a supernova.

question: What causes a pulsar to pulse on and off?

options.A: A neutron star is producing a jet of radiation that is sweeping across the sky.
options.B: A neutron star is expanding and then shrinking.
options.C: A neutron star is spinning slower and then faster.
options.D: A neutron star is collapsing and then exploding.
options.E: Both (b) and (c) simultaneously.

explanation: A pulsar pulses because it is a rapidly rotating neutron star with a strong magnetic field that channels radiation into narrow beams along the magnetic poles. As the star rotates, these beams sweep through space like a lighthouse beacon. We detect a pulse each time a beam points toward Earth; the pulse rate equals the rotation rate of the neutron star.

question: Which of the following is NEVER left behind after a supernova explosion?

options.A: A neutron star.
options.B: A pulsar.
options.C: A black hole.
options.D: A planetary nebula.
options.E: A supernova remnant.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: A supernova explosion leaves behind either a neutron star or a black hole (in the case of core-collapse/Type II supernovae), or nothing (in the case of Type Ia thermonuclear supernovae, which completely destroy the white dwarf). A main-sequence star is never left behind after a supernova — the progenitor star is destroyed in the explosion, with the core compressed into a compact remnant or obliterated entirely.

question: What kinds of objects lie in the disk of our galaxy? (2019)

options.A: open clusters
options.B: O and B stars
options.C: old K and M stars
options.D: gas and dust
options.E: all of the above

explanation: The disk of the Milky Way contains a mix of young and old stars, open clusters, interstellar gas and dust clouds, and the spiral arms. It is the site of ongoing star formation and contains stars of all spectral types currently fusing hydrogen. The thin disk is particularly rich in gas, dust, and young massive stars, while the thick disk contains older, more metal-poor stars.

question: Where does most star formation occur in the Milky Way today? (2019)

options.A: in the halo
options.B: in the bulge
options.C: in the spiral arms
options.D: in the Galactic center
options.E: uniformly throughout the Galaxy

explanation: Star formation in the Milky Way today is predominantly occurring in the spiral arms of the galactic disk, where density waves compress giant molecular clouds triggering gravitational collapse. The presence of young hot O and B stars, HII regions (ionized hydrogen clouds), and molecular clouds in the spiral arms is direct evidence of ongoing star formation. The galactic halo has no significant current star formation.

question: How do we know that halo stars are older, on average, than disk stars?

options.A: Halo stars orbit in random directions but disk stars have more ordered orbits.
options.B: There are no blue halo stars.
options.C: There are no red disk stars.
options.D: Theories of galaxy formation tell us that the halo formed earlier than the disk.
options.E: We see evidence for new stars forming in the disk today.

sub_topic: 16.4 The Stellar Halo and Globular Clusters
explanation: Halo stars are known to be older than disk stars because they have much lower metallicity (lower abundances of elements heavier than helium). In the early universe, only hydrogen and helium were available, so the first stars (Population II, now in the halo) are metal-poor. Later generations of stars (Population I, in the disk) formed from gas enriched by previous stellar generations and thus have higher metallicity.

question: How do we know that there is much more mass in the halo of our galaxy than in the disk?

options.A: There are so many globular clusters in the halo that their total mass is greater than the mass of stars in the disk.
options.B: Stars in the outskirts of the Milky Way orbit the galaxy at much higher speeds than we would expect if all the mass were concentrated in the disk.
options.C: Although the question of mass in the halo was long mysterious, we now know the halo exists because we see so many brown dwarfs.
options.D: The recent discovery of photinos, combined with theoretical predictions, tells us that there must be a huge mass of photinos in the halo.
options.E: We don't know that there is more mass in the halo; it is only a guess based on theory.

explanation: The flat rotation curve of our galaxy shows that the orbital speeds of stars and gas remain roughly constant at large distances from the galactic center, rather than declining as expected if most mass were concentrated in the visible disk. By applying Newton's law of gravity, the total mass within each orbital radius can be calculated, revealing far more mass than is visible — the excess is attributed to a dark matter halo extending well beyond the disk.

question: Gravitational lensing occurs when (2019)

options.A: massive objects bend light beams that are passing nearby.
options.B: massive objects increase the energy of light passing near them, causing a blue-shift, and making distant objects appear much brighter than they normally would.
options.C: dark matter builds up in a particular region of space, leading to a very dense region and an extremely high mass-to-light ratio.
options.D: telescope lenses are distorted by gravity.

explanation: Gravitational lensing is the bending of light by mass — a prediction of General Relativity that has been extensively confirmed. When a massive galaxy or cluster lies between us and a more distant source, the curvature of spacetime around the foreground mass bends light from the background source, producing distorted, magnified, or multiple images. This phenomenon allows astronomers to map mass distributions including invisible dark matter.

question: Which types of galaxies have a clearly defined disk component?

options.A: Spirals
options.B: Ellipticals
options.C: Irregulars only
options.D: Spirals and irregulars
options.E: Any type can have a disk

explanation: Spiral galaxies (including barred spirals) are defined by having a flattened, rotating disk component with spiral arms. Lenticular (S0) galaxies also have a disk but lack prominent spiral arms. Elliptical galaxies are spheroidal with no disk, and irregular galaxies lack regular structure. The presence of a clearly defined disk with spiral structure is the key distinguishing feature of spiral galaxies.

question: Compared to spiral galaxies, elliptical galaxies are (2019)

options.A: redder and rounder.
options.B: redder and flattened.
options.C: bluer and rounder.
options.D: bluer and flattened.
options.E: always much smaller.

explanation: Elliptical galaxies differ from spiral galaxies in that they have little gas and dust, minimal ongoing star formation, and are dominated by old red stars. They lack the disk and spiral arm structure of spirals. Ellipticals are often found in denser environments (galaxy clusters) and are thought to form partly through mergers of spiral galaxies, which disrupt disk structure and consume gas in star formation bursts.

question: Which of the following types of galaxies are most commonly found in large clusters?

options.A: Spirals
options.B: Ellipticals
options.C: Lenticulars
options.D: Irregulars

explanation: Elliptical galaxies are most commonly found in dense galaxy clusters, where the high density of galaxies leads to frequent interactions and mergers. These mergers tend to convert spiral galaxies into ellipticals by disrupting disk structure and exhausting gas supplies. In contrast, spiral galaxies are more common in less dense environments like the outskirts of clusters or in the field.

question: What is Hubble's Law?

options.A: The longer the time period between peaks in brightness, the greater the luminosity of the Cepheid variable star.
options.B: The recession velocity of a galaxy is directly proportional to its distance from us.
options.C: The recession velocity of a galaxy is inversely proportional to its distance from us.
options.D: The faster a spiral galaxy's rotation speed, the more luminous it is.
options.E: The faster a spiral galaxy's rotation speed, the less luminous it is.

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Hubble's Law states that the recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to its distance: v = H₀d, where H₀ is the Hubble constant (~70 km/s/Mpc). This relationship holds for all galaxies on average (after accounting for peculiar velocities) and indicates that the universe is uniformly expanding. The farther a galaxy, the faster it recedes from us — a natural consequence of uniformly expanding space.

question: What is the most accurate way to determine the distance to a nearby star? (2019)

options.A: radar ranging
options.B: stellar parallax
options.C: using Cepheid variables
options.D: Hubble's Law

explanation: Parallax is the most direct and geometrically reliable method for measuring nearby star distances. By observing the apparent shift in a star's position as Earth moves from one side of its orbit to the other (a baseline of 2 AU), the distance can be calculated by simple trigonometry. This method requires no assumptions about the star's intrinsic properties and is thus the most accurate for stars within a few thousand light-years.

question: To date, physicists have investigated the behavior of matter and energy at temperatures as high as those that existed in the universe as far back as _______ after the Big Bang.

options.A: 1 million years
options.B: 300,000 years
options.C: 300 years
options.D: 3 minutes
options.E: 10^-10 seconds

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
sub_topic: 23.5 The Origin of the Big Bang (Unanswered Question)
explanation: Our current physical theories (quantum mechanics and general relativity) have been tested down to temperatures of about 10^15 K and energies accessible at particle colliders. Below the Planck time (~10^-43 seconds after the Big Bang), temperatures and densities were so extreme that quantum gravitational effects dominate and our current theories break down. We do not yet have a complete theory of quantum gravity to describe these conditions.

question: What do we mean by inflation? (2019)

options.A: an increase in mass when nuclei were first formed
options.B: a sudden expansion of the universe just after the Big Bang
options.C: the expansion of the universe that we still observe today
options.D: the sudden release of photons when a particle and antiparticle annihilate each other
options.E: the separation that occurs after two photons collide and create a particle and an antiparticle

topic: TOPIC 23: PROBLEMS WITH THE BIG BANG MODEL
explanation: Cosmic inflation refers to the proposed period of exponentially rapid expansion in the very early universe (around 10^-36 to 10^-32 seconds after the Big Bang). During inflation, a tiny, causally connected patch of the universe was expanded to a scale much larger than the observable universe today. This single mechanism simultaneously explains the horizon problem (uniformity of the CMB), the flatness problem, and the origin of large-scale structure.

question: What kinds of atomic nuclei formed during the era of nucleosynthesis? (2019)

options.A: only hydrogen
options.B: only helium
options.C: hydrogen and helium and trace amounts of deuterium and lithium
options.D: roughly equal amounts of each of the following: hydrogen, helium, deuterium and lithium
options.E: nuclei of all the chemical elements

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.2 Eras of the Universe (Nucleosynthesis, Nuclei, Atoms)
explanation: During the era of nucleosynthesis (roughly the first 3 minutes after the Big Bang), protons and neutrons combined to form atomic nuclei of hydrogen (protons), deuterium, helium-3, helium-4, and trace amounts of lithium-7. The result was approximately 75% hydrogen and 25% helium by mass. Heavier elements were not formed because the universe cooled and expanded too quickly for further fusion.

question: Where do the photons in the cosmic background radiation originate?

options.A: the moment of the Big Bang
options.B: the end of the Planck era
options.C: during the era of nucleosynthesis
options.D: the end of the era of nuclei
options.E: during the era of galaxy formation

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: The photons of the CMB originate from the moment of recombination, about 380,000 years after the Big Bang. Before this time, the universe was an opaque plasma and photons were constantly scattered. When the universe cooled enough for neutral atoms to form, photons decoupled from matter and streamed freely through space. These photons — released from the 'surface of last scattering' — are what we observe today as the CMB.

question: What happens to the light from the Cosmic Microwave Background as it travels to us?

options.A: cosmological expansion causes it to become redshifted
options.B: the peculiar velocity of the Milky-Way causes it to become redshifted
options.C: it fades as it decays into neutrinos
options.D: most of it is absorbed by gas and dust. Only microwave light makes it through
options.E: it changes very little, since the Universe is nearly transparent

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
explanation: As CMB photons travel toward us through expanding space, their wavelengths are stretched by the cosmological expansion — a process called cosmological redshift. The photons originally had wavelengths corresponding to ~3,000 K (visible/near-infrared light), but after traveling through ~13.4 billion years of cosmic expansion, they have been redshifted to microwave wavelengths corresponding to ~2.725 K.

question: What are peculiar velocities? (2019)

options.A: velocities perpendicular to our line of sight
options.B: velocities directly along our line of sight
options.C: velocities that we cannot explain by only the force of gravity
options.D: velocities caused by the expansion of the universe
options.E: velocities of distant objects that are not caused by the expansion of the universe

topic: TOPIC 20: HUBBLE'S LAW & THE BIG BANG
explanation: Peculiar velocities are the motions of galaxies through space that are not due to the Hubble expansion — they represent actual gravitational motions toward mass concentrations. Peculiar velocities are typically a few hundred km/s, which is small compared to the recession velocities of distant galaxies (thousands to hundreds of thousands of km/s) but can dominate for very nearby galaxies.

question: What might be causing the universe to accelerate? (2019)

options.A: WIMPs
options.B: Neutrinos
options.C: white-dwarf supernovae
options.D: dark gravity
options.E: We don't know! — but we call it 'dark energy.'

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.6 Dark Energy and the Cosmological Constant
explanation: The accelerating expansion of the universe is attributed to dark energy — an unknown form of energy with negative pressure that pervades all of space. The simplest model of dark energy is the cosmological constant (Λ), originally introduced by Einstein as a static term in his field equations. Dark energy currently accounts for ~68% of the total energy content of the universe.

question: The expand/collapse version of the Cyclical Universe is

options.A: wrong, because evidence for Hubble's Law show that the Universe is expanding
options.B: wrong, because observations of Type I Supernovae show that the expansion of the Universe is accelerating, not slowing down
options.C: wrong, because no one can figure out how to make a 'Big Crunch' rebound into a Big Bang
options.D: wrong, because an infinitely old Universe doesn't make any sense
options.E: consistent with our best current observations

topic: TOPIC 22: THE ULTIMATE FATE OF THE UNIVERSE
explanation: The expand/collapse cyclical model proposes that the universe expands from a Big Bang, reaches a maximum size, contracts under gravity in a Big Crunch, and then 'bounces' into a new Big Bang — repeating indefinitely. This scenario requires sufficient matter density to reverse the expansion and no dark energy. In our actual universe, dark energy drives accelerating expansion, making a Big Crunch unlikely.

question: Imagine you are floating in the Universe shortly after the Universe has cooled enough for the plasma to become gas, and well before the first stars form. You look around. What do you see?

options.A: An yellow/orange/reddish glow coming from all directions.
options.B: Nothing. Hydrogen and Helium gases are transparent.
options.C: A searing blue glow from the Big Bang, coming from all directions.
options.D: Stars and galaxies in the far distance.
options.E: Dust and other bits of matter from the Big Bang.

topic: TOPIC 21: THE BIG BANG MODEL & DARK ENERGY
sub_topic: 21.2 Eras of the Universe (Nucleosynthesis, Nuclei, Atoms)
explanation: Shortly after recombination (~380,000 years after the Big Bang), the universe became transparent and was filled with a uniform, glowing fog of hydrogen and helium gas. There were no stars, galaxies, or heavy elements — only the slight density fluctuations that would eventually grow into cosmic structure. This period before the first stars formed is sometimes called the 'cosmic dark ages'.

question: If two black holes, each about twenty times the mass of the Sun, were to collide, what would be the result?

options.A: A spectacular explosion called a gamma-ray burst.
options.B: A single black hole with a larger event horizon than either of the two initial ones.
options.C: A supermassive black hole.
options.D: A neutron star.
options.E: A new black hole with a much smaller event horizon than either of the previous ones, due to the much stronger force of gravity of the new black hole.

explanation: When two black holes merge, the event releases an enormous amount of energy as gravitational waves — ripples in spacetime that propagate outward at the speed of light. LIGO (the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) first detected gravitational waves from a black hole merger in 2015 (GW150914). The merger produces a characteristic 'chirp' signal and results in a single larger black hole.

question: Which of the following is experiencing the least acceleration?

options.A: A car traveling along a twisty road exactly at the speed limit.
options.B: The Voyager spacecraft, traveling at 61,000 km/hr, 130 AU from the sun.
options.C: An airplane heading down the runway for takeoff.
options.D: A giggling child being bounced on his mother's knee.
options.E: A roller coaster at the bottom of a loop.

explanation: Einstein's equivalence principle states that being in free fall is equivalent to being weightless — an object in free fall experiences no local gravitational effects. An astronaut in a freely falling spaceship (like the ISS in orbit) experiences no acceleration in their local frame — they are in free fall and feel weightless. Objects on Earth's surface are NOT in free fall; they are being pushed up by the ground and experience 1g of acceleration.

question: Two cars approach each other on a highway. One is traveling at 90 km/hr. The other is traveling in the opposite direction at 80 km/hr. What is the speed of the faster car, relative to the other car?

options.A: 0 km/hr
options.B: 10 km/hr
options.C: 90 km/hr
options.D: 80 km/hr
options.E: 170 km/hr

explanation: In special relativity, the velocities of objects do not simply add in the classical way when relative speeds approach c. However, for everyday objects like cars, classical Galilean addition works perfectly: two cars approaching each other at 90 km/hr and 110 km/hr have a closing speed of 200 km/hr relative to the ground. The relativistic velocity addition formula only becomes significant when speeds are a substantial fraction of c.

question: You and Al are both floating freely in your spaceships. Suppose Al is moving away from you at 85 km/hr. You throw a ball in his direction at a speed of 75 km/hr. According to Al, which of the following is going on?

options.A: He sees you standing still and the ball moving towards him at 75 km/hr.
options.B: He sees you moving away at 85 km/hr and the ball moving towards him at 75 km/hr.
options.C: He sees you moving away at 85 km/hr and the ball moving towards him at 10 km/hr.
options.D: He sees you moving away at 85 km/hr and the ball moving away at 10 km/hr.
options.E: He sees you moving towards him at 85 km/hr and the ball moving toward him at 75 km/hr.

explanation: In special relativity, all motion is relative — there is no preferred reference frame. If Al is moving away from you at constant velocity, you could equally say you are moving away from Al, or that Al is moving away from you. Neither perspective is more correct than the other; both are valid inertial reference frames. The laws of physics, including the speed of light, are the same in both frames.

question: A spaceship is moving past us at a speed close to the speed of light. How would we view time (clocks) on the spaceship as it goes by?

options.A: Time is the same for everyone.
options.B: Their clocks are ticking backwards.
options.C: Their clocks are going at the same rate as ours.
options.D: Their clocks are going faster than ours.
options.E: Their clocks are going slower than ours.

explanation: Observers who watch a spaceship moving at near-light speed would see two effects: time dilation (clocks on the ship running more slowly) and length contraction (the ship appearing shorter along its direction of motion). From the Earth-based observer's perspective, time on the ship passes more slowly and the ship is compressed along its direction of travel. Both effects are real and consistent within special relativity.

question: Ben is racing a light beam in a 100-meter dash. Ben bursts out of the starting blocks at 0.99c. At the same instant, a flashlight beam is turned on. According to Ben, what happens?

options.A: The light beam gradually pulls ahead of him and wins the race because it is going 1 percent of the speed of light faster than him.
options.B: The light beam instantly finishes the race, before Ben even has a chance to start.
options.C: The light beam moves out ahead of him at the full speed of light, winning the race easily.
options.D: Time stops for Ben and he never finishes the race.
options.E: Ben wins the race and becomes a hero.

sub_topic: 2.2 Constancy of the Speed of Light
explanation: No material object can reach or exceed the speed of light. As an object accelerates toward c, its relativistic momentum increases without bound, requiring ever-greater energy for ever-smaller additional speed gains. Ben, starting at 0.99c, can never catch or outrun a light beam — the light beam always moves at exactly c relative to Ben regardless of Ben's speed. The gap between Ben's speed and c never closes.

question: Einstein's equivalence principle says that:

options.A: Everyone measures the speed of light to be equivalent.
options.B: The effects of gravity are exactly equivalent to the effects of acceleration.
options.C: Someone traveling at 0.9c will age at the same rate as someone at 0.99c.
options.D: What we perceive as the force of gravity in fact arises from the curvature of space and time.
options.E: All people see themselves at an equivalent distance to the centre of the universe.

explanation: Einstein's equivalence principle states that the effects of uniform gravitational acceleration and inertial acceleration are locally indistinguishable. An observer in a closed box cannot determine by local experiments whether they are at rest in a gravitational field or accelerating in empty space. This equivalence was the key insight that led Einstein from special relativity to the general theory of relativity.

question: What do we mean by the event horizon of a black hole?

options.A: It is the centre of the black hole.
options.B: It is the 'bottomless pit' of the black hole.
options.C: It is the place where time begins to slow down as you approach a black hole.
options.D: It is the boundary within which events in the black hole cannot influence events in the outside universe.
options.E: It is the surface of the black hole.

explanation: The event horizon of a black hole is the boundary at a distance equal to the Schwarzschild radius from the singularity, at which the escape speed equals the speed of light. Any object or signal inside the event horizon cannot escape because it would need to travel faster than light. The event horizon is not a physical surface — it is an invisible boundary in spacetime with profound consequences for anything that crosses it.

question: If the moon were to be replaced with a lunar mass black hole, what would happen?

options.A: We wouldn't be able to see the Moon.
options.B: Tides would become much larger.
options.C: Satellites would be thrown out of their orbits.
options.D: Over a few hundred years, the Earth would be sucked into the black hole.
options.E: The Earth will begin orbiting around the Moon.

explanation: If the Moon were replaced by a lunar-mass black hole at the same distance, the gravitational field at Earth's location would be identical — the same mass at the same distance produces the same gravity. Earth would continue to orbit as normal, and the Moon's tidal effects on Earth's oceans would be the same. The only difference is that we wouldn't be able to see the black hole and it would be very dangerous to approach closely.

question: Which of the following statements about electrons is FALSE?

options.A: Electrons orbit the nucleus similar to how planets orbit the Sun.
options.B: Within an atom, an electron can have only particular energies.
options.C: Electrons can jump between energy levels in an atom only if they receive or give up an amount of energy equal to the difference in energy between the energy levels.
options.D: An electron has a negative electrical charge.
options.E: Electrons have very little mass compared to protons or neutrons.

explanation: Electrons in atoms occupy discrete energy levels and can absorb or emit photons as they transition between levels. A FALSE statement about electrons would be claiming they can exist at any energy level between quantized states — in reality, only discrete (quantized) energy levels are allowed. Electrons also have extremely small mass (about 1/1836 the mass of a proton) and carry negative electric charge.

question: Which of the following statements about a star's spectrum is FALSE?

options.A: The peak of the star's thermal emission tells us its temperature: Hotter stars peak at shorter (bluer) wavelengths.
options.B: The total amount of light in the spectrum tells us the star's radius.
options.C: We can identify chemical elements present in the star by recognizing patterns of spectral lines that correspond to particular chemicals.
options.D: We can look at Doppler shifts of spectral lines to determine the star's speed toward or away from us.
options.E: A star's spectrum could contain absorption lines from several different kinds of atoms and molecules.

explanation: Stellar spectra reveal surface temperature (from spectral type), chemical composition (from absorption line patterns), and radial velocity (from Doppler shifts). A FALSE statement about stellar spectra would be claiming they reveal the star's interior composition directly — spectra only sample the outermost atmospheric layers. The interior composition can only be inferred from stellar models and neutrino observations.

question: At the centre of the Sun, fusion converts hydrogen into:

options.A: hydrogen compounds.
options.B: plasma.
options.C: radiation and elements like carbon and nitrogen.
options.D: radioactive elements like uranium and plutonium.
options.E: helium, energy, and neutrinos.

explanation: At the Sun's core, the proton-proton chain converts four hydrogen nuclei (protons) into one helium-4 nucleus. The helium-4 nucleus (also called an alpha particle) consists of two protons and two neutrons, with the two excess protons having been converted to neutrons via beta-plus decay (emitting positrons and neutrinos). This is the net result: 4H → He-4 + 2 positrons + 2 neutrinos + energy.

question: Neutrinos are so difficult to detect because they:

options.A: are so rare.
options.B: have no mass.
options.C: move at nearly the speed of light.
options.D: rarely interact with matter.
options.E: are so small.

explanation: Neutrinos interact with matter only through the weak nuclear force (and gravity), making them extraordinarily difficult to detect. They can pass through the entire Earth with almost no chance of interaction — trillions of solar neutrinos pass through your body every second without effect. To detect them, experiments like the Sudbury Neutrino Observatory use enormous tanks of water or heavy water surrounded by photomultiplier tubes, looking for the rare neutrino interactions.

question: If the distance between us and a star is doubled, with everything else remaining the same, the luminosity:

options.A: remains the same, but the apparent brightness is decreased by a factor of two.
options.B: remains the same, but the apparent brightness is decreased by a factor of four.
options.C: is decreased by a factor of four, and the apparent brightness is decreased by a factor of four.
options.D: is decreased by a factor of four, but the apparent brightness remains the same.
options.E: is decreased by a factor of two, and the apparent brightness is decreased by a factor of two.

sub_topic: 9.3 The Inverse Square Law of Light
explanation: The inverse square law states that apparent brightness scales as 1/d². If the distance to a star doubles (d → 2d), the apparent brightness drops by a factor of 4 (2² = 4), since the same luminosity is spread over four times the area. The star would appear one-quarter as bright as before. Distance and brightness are the two key variables in this fundamental relationship.

question: Repeated observations of Star A show it moves back and forth by 0.1" relative to more distant background stars. Star B only moves back and forth by 0.05". When compared to Star A, Star B is:

options.A: further away from Earth.
options.B: closer to Earth.
options.C: orbiting the centre of the galaxy at a faster speed.
options.D: orbiting the centre of the galaxy at a slower speed.
options.E: half as luminous.

explanation: The back-and-forth motion of a star observed over the course of a year is the parallax effect — the apparent shift in position due to Earth's annual orbit around the Sun. A parallax angle of 0.1 arcseconds corresponds to a distance of 10 parsecs (since d = 1/p in parsecs when p is in arcseconds). This direct geometric method is the foundation of all other distance measurements.

question: On a Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, where would we find stars that are cool and luminous? (2015)

options.A: upper right
options.B: lower right
options.C: upper left
options.D: lower left
options.E: centre

explanation: Stars that are cool (low surface temperature) and luminous (high total power output) must have very large radii to compensate for their low temperature per unit area. These stars plot in the upper-right region of the HR diagram. Cool, luminous stars are red giants and supergiants — evolved stars that have expanded dramatically from the main sequence.

question: Star A is more luminous and redder than the Sun. Which of the following statements CANNOT be confirmed with this information?

options.A: Star A is cooler than the Sun.
options.B: Star A is larger than the Sun.
options.C: Star A is more massive than the Sun.
options.D: Star A is performing nuclear fusion outside of the core.
options.E: Star A is no longer on the main sequence.

explanation: Star A is more luminous and redder (cooler) than the Sun, placing it in the upper-right of the HR diagram — the giant or supergiant region. A statement that CANNOT be true is that Star A is on the main sequence, since main-sequence stars follow a tight mass-luminosity-temperature relationship and a cooler-than-Sun star on the main sequence would be less luminous than the Sun. Being more luminous and cooler requires a much larger radius than a main-sequence star.

question: You observe a star in the disk of the Milky Way, and you want to plot the star on an H-R diagram. Which of the following properties is NOT necessary to complete this task? (2015)

options.A: The spectral type of the star.
options.B: The distance to the star.
options.C: The apparent brightness of the star in our sky.
options.D: The rotation rate of the star.

explanation: An HR diagram requires both luminosity and surface temperature to plot a star. Luminosity requires knowing apparent brightness and distance (d from parallax or other methods), and surface temperature is inferred from spectral type. Without knowing the distance, you cannot convert apparent brightness to luminosity, so you cannot place the star accurately on the HR diagram.

question: Which statement is true for main sequence stars?

options.A: O type stars are longer lived than M type stars.
options.B: O type stars have a lower surface temperature than M type stars.
options.C: O type stars appear bluer than M type stars.
options.D: O type stars are less massive than M type stars.
options.E: O type stars have strong molecular lines.

explanation: A defining property of main-sequence stars is that more massive ones are both hotter and more luminous — the main sequence is a mass sequence. Main-sequence stars fuse hydrogen in their cores and are in hydrostatic equilibrium. The true statement is that main-sequence stars show a correlation between mass, luminosity, and temperature: more massive main-sequence stars are hotter, more luminous, and have shorter lifetimes.

question: Which main sequence stars have the longest lifetime?

options.A: Blue stars, because they have more hydrogen to convert to helium.
options.B: Blue stars, because they have lower luminosity, and burn their fuel more slowly.
options.C: Red stars, because they have more hydrogen to convert to helium.
options.D: Red stars, because they have lower luminosity, and burn their fuel more slowly.
options.E: Their appearance has nothing to do with their lifetimes.

explanation: Main-sequence lifetime scales approximately as M^(-2.5), meaning lower-mass stars live much longer than high-mass stars. The lowest-mass main-sequence stars — red M-dwarf stars — have lifetimes of tens to hundreds of billions of years, far longer than the current age of the universe. This extreme longevity occurs because they are very dim and consume their hydrogen fuel at an extraordinarily slow rate.

question: The following are stages in the life of a solar mass star: 1: Hydrogen shell burning, 2: Helium core burning, 3: White Dwarf, 4: Hydrogen core burning, 5: Helium shell burning. Which is the correct order?
topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: The life stages of a 1 solar mass star in order are: main sequence (hydrogen core fusion) → hydrogen shell burning (subgiant/red giant ascent) → helium core fusion (horizontal branch) → helium shell burning (asymptotic giant branch) → planetary nebula → white dwarf. The correct ordering of the given stages must follow this evolutionary sequence, with hydrogen shell burning preceding helium core fusion.

question: The following stages in the life of a solar mass star: 1: H shell burning, 2: He core burning, 3: White Dwarf, 4: H core burning, 5: He shell burning. In which stages does the star spend the MOST amount of time?

options.A: 2 is longest. 5 is second longest.
options.B: 4 is longest. 2 is second longest.
options.C: 3 is longest. 1 is second longest.
options.D: 4 is longest. 5 is second longest.
options.E: 3 is longest. 4 is second longest.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: For a 1 solar mass star, the sequence proceeds from: hydrogen shell burning (star ascends red giant branch) → helium core burning (horizontal branch, after helium flash) → helium shell burning (asymptotic giant branch) → mass loss → planetary nebula → white dwarf. The helium flash marks the violent ignition of degenerate helium, after which the star settles into steady helium core fusion on the horizontal branch.

question: In the life of a high mass star, core fusion stops when the star has an iron core. Which of the following is the best explanation for this?

options.A: Iron is the densest element – and so the core collapses to form a neutron star or a black hole.
options.B: The electrons in the iron core approach the speed of light, causing a failure of electron degeneracy pressure before iron fusion can begin.
options.C: Iron is less dense than some other elements, so it floats up, and never really forms an iron core.
options.D: Iron has the lowest mass per nucleon. Fusion of iron takes energy – it doesn't release it.
options.E: The pressure on the core and the core's temperature are inadequate to start iron fusion.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: When a massive star's iron core grows beyond the Chandrasekhar mass (~1.4 solar masses), electron degeneracy pressure can no longer support it and the core collapses in milliseconds. The infalling material bounces off the incompressible neutron star that forms, sending a shockwave outward through the still-falling outer layers. This shockwave, energized by neutrinos from the core, ejects the outer layers in a Type II supernova.

question: What is a planetary nebula? (2015)

options.A: A disk of gas surrounding a protostar that may form into planets.
options.B: What is left of the planets around a star after a low-mass star has ended its life.
options.C: The expanding shell of gas that is no longer gravitationally held to the remnant of a low-mass star.
options.D: The molecular cloud from which protostars form.
options.E: The expanding shell of gas that is left when a white dwarf explodes as a supernova.

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: A planetary nebula is the expelled outer envelope of a low-mass dying star, illuminated by ultraviolet radiation from the hot stellar core (the proto-white-dwarf) left behind. The name is historically misleading — William Herschel thought they resembled the disks of planets through his telescope, but they have nothing to do with planets. They are glowing shells of gas that expand into the interstellar medium over thousands of years.

question: What types of stars end their lives with supernovae?

options.A: All stars that are red in colour.
options.B: All stars that are yellow in colour.
options.C: Stars that are at least several times the mass of the Sun.
options.D: Stars that are similar in mass to the Sun.
options.E: Stars that have reached an age of 10 billion years.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: Core-collapse (Type II) supernovae occur when massive stars (above ~8 solar masses) form an iron core that collapses. Low-mass stars like the Sun do not have sufficient mass to reach the temperatures needed for iron core formation; they end as white dwarfs via planetary nebulae. Only stars with initial masses above approximately 8 solar masses undergo core collapse and explode as Type II supernovae.

question: Which of the following statements about neutron stars is FALSE?

options.A: The formation of neutron stars produces a supernova.
options.B: Neutron stars are the last stage of life for many high mass stars.
options.C: Neutron stars weigh more than the Sun, but are the size of the Earth.
options.D: Neutron stars often spin many times per second.
options.E: Neutron stars have very strong magnetic fields.

explanation: Neutron stars are incredibly dense objects — a teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh hundreds of millions of tonnes. They have radii of only about 10 km but masses of 1.4–2+ solar masses, meaning their density is comparable to atomic nuclear density (~10^14 g/cm³). A FALSE statement about neutron stars would be that they are similar in size to white dwarfs — white dwarfs are roughly Earth-sized (~10,000 km radius), not ~10 km like neutron stars.

question: When a star is undergoing Helium shell burning, what is the star's core mostly composed of?

options.A: Helium
options.B: Hydrogen
options.C: Carbon
options.D: Oxygen
options.E: Iron

topic: TOPIC 13: LIFE CYCLE OF A SUN-LIKE STAR
explanation: During the asymptotic giant branch (AGB) phase, a low-mass star alternates between hydrogen shell burning and helium shell burning. When the star is in the helium shell burning phase, the helium shell burns outward and eventually exhausts the available helium, at which point hydrogen shell burning resumes and builds up a new helium layer. During helium shell burning, the core below the helium shell is composed of carbon and oxygen — the ashes of helium fusion.

question: If the mass of a neutron star increases, what eventually happens?

options.A: You get a supermassive neutron star, like in the centre of a globular cluster.
options.B: The neutron star blows itself apart in a Type Ia supernova.
options.C: The escape velocity on the surface exceeds the speed of light, and the neutron star becomes a black hole.
options.D: The velocity of the electrons in the core approaches the speed of light, and the neutron star becomes a white dwarf.

sub_topic: 15.3 Stellar-Mass Black Holes from Massive Stars
explanation: If the mass of a neutron star increases (e.g., by accreting material from a binary companion), it approaches the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit (~2–3 solar masses). Above this limit, neutron degeneracy pressure can no longer support the star against gravity, and it collapses further into a stellar-mass black hole. This is one pathway by which black holes can form after the initial supernova has already created a neutron star.

question: The luminosity of a supernova is comparable to the luminosity of:

options.A: the Sun.
options.B: a galaxy.
options.C: the brightest stars.
options.D: a hydrogen bomb.
options.E: the rest of the observable universe.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
sub_topic: 14.4 Core Collapse and Type II Supernova
explanation: At peak brightness, a supernova can be as luminous as an entire galaxy — billions of stars combined. Type Ia supernovae have a characteristic peak luminosity of about 10^43 watts, roughly equal to the total luminosity of a large spiral galaxy for a few weeks. This extraordinary luminosity makes supernovae visible across billions of light-years and makes them useful as standard candles for measuring cosmic distances.

question: Where was most of the iron in the Earth produced?

options.A: In the cores of massive stars at the end of their lives.
options.B: In the envelopes of M dwarfs.
options.C: In the envelopes of red giant stars.
options.D: In the cores of planets during planet formation.
options.E: In supernovae.

topic: TOPIC 14: LIFE CYCLE OF A MASSIVE STAR
explanation: Iron is produced in the cores of massive stars through silicon burning (the final fusion stage), which builds up the iron core that eventually triggers a Type II supernova. The iron in the Earth was synthesized in the cores of massive stars that lived and died billions of years before the Solar System formed, and was dispersed into the interstellar medium by supernova explosions. We are, quite literally, made of star stuff.

question: Which of the following statements about pulsars is FALSE?

options.A: They are neutron stars.
options.B: They spin very rapidly.
options.C: They are observed through very regular bursts of radio waves.
options.D: They have very strong magnetic fields.
options.E: Their typical luminosity is the same as the sun.

explanation: Pulsars are rotating neutron stars that emit beams of electromagnetic radiation. A FALSE statement about pulsars would be that all neutron stars are observed as pulsars — in fact, we only see a neutron star as a pulsar when its beam happens to sweep across Earth. Many neutron stars exist that we cannot detect as pulsars because their beams never point toward us ('alignment problem').

question: About how many stars are in the Milky-Way Galaxy?

options.A: 2 thousand
options.B: 200 thousand
options.C: 200 million
options.D: 200 billion
options.E: 200 trillion

sub_topic: 16.1 Our Galaxy as a Barred Spiral
explanation: The Milky Way galaxy contains roughly 200–400 billion stars, typically cited as approximately 200–300 billion. Most of these are faint, low-mass red dwarf stars that are individually invisible to the naked eye. The total number is estimated by measuring the total luminosity and mass of the galaxy and dividing by typical stellar values.

question: Which part of the Milky Way Galaxy has the largest concentration of gas and dust?

options.A: Bulge
options.B: Disk
options.C: Halo
options.D: Dark matter halo
options.E: Galactic centre

explanation: The galactic disk contains the highest concentration of gas and dust in the Milky Way, particularly in the spiral arms within the thin disk. Giant molecular clouds (GMCs) are the largest gas structures, found predominantly along the spiral arms. The halo contains essentially no gas or dust, and the bulge has some gas but far less than the disk's spiral arms.

question: Which parts of the Milky Way Galaxy have most globular clusters?

options.A: Bulge
options.B: Disk
options.C: Halo
options.D: Dark matter halo
options.E: Galactic centre

sub_topic: 16.4 The Stellar Halo and Globular Clusters
explanation: Globular clusters are found predominantly in the stellar halo of the Milky Way — a roughly spherical region extending to ~100,000 light-years from the galactic center. The Milky Way has about 150 known globular clusters distributed in this halo. They are ancient, metal-poor clusters of 100,000 to several million old stars that formed early in the galaxy's history.

question: Which parts of the Milky Way Galaxy have the most O and B stars?

options.A: Bulge
options.B: Disk
options.C: Halo
options.D: Dark matter halo
options.E: Galactic centre

explanation: O and B stars (the hottest, most massive, and most luminous stars) are found predominantly in the spiral arms of the galactic disk because they form there and have very short lifetimes (millions of years) — too short to migrate far from their birthplace. Their presence in the spiral arms is direct evidence of ongoing star formation in those regions. The halo and bulge have very few O and B stars.

question: In systems that follow a Keplerian rotation curve,

options.A: Objects close to the centre of the system move faster than objects further away.
options.B: Objects close to the centre of the system are always more massive than objects further away.
options.C: Mass is distributed evenly throughout the system.
options.D: Objects all must orbit in the same plane.
options.E: Objects far from the centre of the system all move at the same speed.

explanation: In a Keplerian system (like the Solar System), where most mass is concentrated at the center, orbital speed decreases with distance as v ∝ 1/√r. This is because the enclosed mass remains essentially constant as you move outward, so gravity weakens. Planets farther from the Sun move more slowly — Mercury is much faster than Neptune — perfectly following Kepler's Third Law.

question: What fraction of the mass of the Milky Way is dark matter?
explanation: Based on rotation curve analysis and other measurements, approximately 90% of the Milky Way's total mass is in the form of dark matter, with normal (baryonic) matter comprising only about 10%. This estimate comes from comparing the observed flat rotation curve to what would be expected from the visible mass alone. The dark matter is distributed in a roughly spherical halo extending far beyond the visible galaxy.

question: Which is TRUE? (2015)

options.A: Elliptical galaxies have a distinct disk. Spiral galaxies do not.
options.B: Spiral galaxies have far more O and B stars than elliptical galaxies.
options.C: Elliptical galaxies have higher concentrations of gas and dust than spiral galaxies.
options.D: Spiral galaxies are thought to be the result of the merger of Elliptical galaxies.
options.E: Elliptical galaxies have more young stars than spiral galaxies.

explanation: Galaxy mergers and interactions play a major role in galaxy evolution over cosmic time. When galaxies collide, gas clouds compress and trigger bursts of star formation, and the gravitational disturbances can transform spiral galaxies into ellipticals. Evidence for this includes observed interacting galaxy pairs, tidal tails, and the correlation between elliptical galaxy prevalence and dense cluster environments.

question: Suppose that we look at a photograph of many galaxies. Assuming that all galaxies formed at about the same time, which galaxy are we seeing when it was the youngest?

options.A: The one that is reddest in colour.
options.B: The one that is bluest in colour.
options.C: The one that is farthest away.
options.D: The one that is closest to us.
options.E: The one that appears smallest in size

explanation: More distant galaxies appear as they were in the past, because light takes time to travel to us.
